Google Cloud Professional Cloud Architect

A complete study hub aligned to the official v6.1 blueprint. Learn the service-selection judgment the questions test, master the four current case studies, then drill all 500 questions until every answer is reflex. The bar here is not the ~70% pass mark — aim for 100%.

50–60
Questions
2 hr
Time
~30–40%
Case-study tied
100%
Your target (pass ~70%)
⚠ Exam updated Oct 30, 2025 (v6.1). Mountkirk Games, TerramEarth & Helicopter Racing League are retired. Current four: EHR Healthcare, Altostrat Media, Cymbal Retail, KnightMotives Automotive — three are GenAI-centric. New: Vertex AI sections (2.4/2.5), the Well-Architected Framework, and Migration Center. Also note: Deployment Manager hits end-of-support Mar 31 2026 (use Terraform/Infrastructure Manager).

3-day plan

Day 1 — Today

Read Compute + Storage & Databases. Memorize the keyword→service triggers. Take the quiz cold; review every miss.

Day 2

Networking, Security, Ops, GenAI & ML. Read all four case-study pages and answer their prep questions.

Day 3 — Eve

Re-take the quiz filtered to weak domains until you hit 100%. Re-skim every trap. Morning of: read the four official case-study PDFs once more.

The mental model that wins "best answer" questions

  1. Match the stated priority. The executive statement ranks the real goal — cost, reliability, compliance, speed. Serve it.
  2. Prefer the most managed option that meets the requirement ("minimize operational overhead" → serverless/managed).
  3. Don't over-engineer. Single-region need → don't pick the global/expensive service (Spanner is the classic cost trap).
  4. Eliminate two obviously wrong, then decide the last two on priority.

Video drilling (watch + answer along)

Exam-question walkthrough playlists. Pause before each answer, decide, then check — same discipline as the quiz here.

On the case studies: the four current ones (EHR, Altostrat, Cymbal, KnightMotives) changed in Oct 2025, so few videos cover them yet. Use the full official text on each case page here plus this current written guide: Mastering PCA Case Study Questions (2026).

More resources

Official certification page · Exam guide v6.1 (PDF) · Google Skills learning path · Go Cloud Architects free study guide

Avoid braindumps. Sites selling "real exam questions, word for word" violate Google's exam terms — using them risks decertification and a ban, and they do not build the judgment the exam tests. Stick to original practice questions (like this hub) and official materials.
Built on PCA exam guide v6.1 (verified June 2026). Service mappings are best-practice guidance, not official answer keys. Verify at cloud.google.com/certification.
Exam guide v6.1

The Official Blueprint

The exam is organized into six sections. Every quiz question maps to one of these; the domain pages give you the services, and the case studies are where sections 1, 3 and 5 get tested in context.

#SectionWhat it testsStudy pages
1Designing & planning a cloud solution architectureBusiness/technical requirements, service selection, blueprints, cost/ROI, networking & storage designAll domains + Case Studies
2Managing & provisioning the solution infrastructureCompute/storage/network/DB provisioning, IaC, Vertex AI (2.4) & pre-built APIs (2.5)Compute · Data · GenAI & ML
3Designing for security & complianceIAM, encryption, network security, VPC-SC, compliance, residency, securing AISecurity & IAM
4Analyzing & optimizing technical & business processesCost optimization, performance, monitoring-driven improvement, business processOperations & Cost · WAF
5Managing implementationMigration planning & execution, change management, dev/test/prod, APIsWAF & Migration
6Ensuring solution & operations reliabilityObservability, SLO/SLI/error budgets, DR, Operational Excellence pillarOperations & Cost
The Well-Architected Framework (6 pillars: Operational Excellence, Security/Privacy/Compliance, Reliability, Cost, Performance, Sustainability) is now a lens applied across all six sections. See the WAF & Migration page.
Domain · Compute

Compute

Pick the runtime by trading control against operational overhead. Choose the least-ops option that still meets the requirement.

Decision tree

Containerized?
├─ NO
│  ├─ OS control / licensing / lift-and-shift / custom kernel ─→ Compute Engine
│  ├─ Event-driven snippet (Pub/Sub, Storage trigger) ────────→ Cloud Run functions
│  └─ Managed web app, supported runtime, no Docker ──────────→ App Engine Standard
└─ YES
   ├─ Single stateless container, scale-to-zero, request/event ─→ Cloud Run
   └─ Orchestration / many services / mesh / DaemonSets
      ├─ Hands-off, pay per pod ────────────────────────────────→ GKE Autopilot
      └─ Node-level control / custom node config ───────────────→ GKE Standard

Serverless & GKE

ServiceScale to 0Best for
Cloud Run functions (was Cloud Functions)YesEvent-driven glue, lightweight HTTP, triggers
Cloud RunYesStateless containers, APIs, any language; revisions + % traffic split for canary/rollback
App Engine StandardYesManaged web apps, fast cold start, traffic splitting, spikes
App Engine FlexibleNo (min 1)Custom runtimes, background processes, large deps
GKE Autopilot—Full K8s API, no node mgmt, billed per pod request
GKE Standard—Node-level control, DaemonSets, custom hardware, bin-packing

Scaling on GKE: HPA scales replicas (supports custom/external metrics like RPS via Cloud Monitoring); VPA resizes pods; cluster autoscaler adds nodes when pods are unschedulable. Regional clusters = HA control plane + nodes across zones.

Compute Engine

Pricing & licensing

  • Spot VMs (replaced Preemptible): up to ~60–91% off, reclaimable → fault-tolerant batch in a MIG, checkpoint to GCS.
  • CUD: commit 1/3 yr → steady 24/7. Flexible CUD now spans CE/GKE/Cloud Run.
  • Sustained Use Discounts: automatic, no commitment.
  • Sole-tenant nodes: dedicated physical host → BYOL per-core / isolation. (Confidential VM ≠ isolation — it encrypts memory but shares hardware.)
  • Custom machine types: odd vCPU:RAM ratio.

Managed Instance Groups

  • Instance template = immutable blueprint (required).
  • Autohealing recreates failed VMs (health-check driven).
  • Regional MIG spans zones → survives a zone failure.
  • Rolling updates / canary; autoscaling on CPU, LB capacity, schedule, or Monitoring metric.
  • Stateful MIG preserves per-instance disks/IPs.
  • Compact placement policy → lowest inter-node latency (HPC).

Keyword → service

  • "Lift-and-shift / existing license / OS control"Compute Engine
  • "Run code, scale to zero, single container"Cloud Run
  • "Microservices / mesh / DaemonSets / portable"GKE (Anthos)
  • "Reduce ops, full K8s API, pay per pod"GKE Autopilot
  • "Managed web app, traffic splitting, no Docker"App Engine Std
  • "Fault-tolerant batch, cheapest"Spot VMs in MIG
  • "Steady 24/7 predictable, lowest cost"CUD
  • "Physical isolation / per-core BYOL"Sole-tenant
Traps: App Engine Flexible & Cloud Run w/ min-instances don't scale to zero. Spot VMs never for stateful/always-on. "Recreate failed VMs" = MIG autohealing. "Scale on requests-per-second" = HPA custom/external metric, not cluster autoscaler.
Domain · Storage & Databases

Storage & Databases

The heaviest-tested domain. Database selection is pattern-matching on four axes: relational vs NoSQL, regional vs global, OLTP vs OLAP, and scale.

Database selection matrix

ServiceModelScopePick when (keywords)
Cloud SQLRelationalRegionalManaged MySQL/Postgres/SQL Server; lift-and-shift OLTP
AlloyDBPostgres / HTAPRegionalPostgres but need speed/scale; analytics on transactional data; Oracle→OSS
SpannerRelational, distributedGlobalglobal + strong consistency + relational + horizontal scale; 99.999% multi-region
Firestore (Native)Document NoSQLRegional/MultiMobile/web app, real-time sync, offline SDK
BigtableWide-columnRegionaltime-series / IoT / high throughput / low-latency key lookups
BigQueryColumnar OLAPMulti-regionanalytics / warehouse / BI / ad-hoc SQL over TB–PB
MemorystoreIn-memory (Redis)RegionalCache / session / sub-ms / leaderboard
#1 trap — Spanner over-selection: only when global + strong consistency + relational + horizontal. Single-region relational = Cloud SQL. BigQuery = analytical SQL; Bigtable = operational per-key reads/writes — never swap them.

HA, scaling & data movement

  • Cloud SQL HA = regional synchronous standby + auto failover. Read replicas ≠ HA (scale reads).
  • BigQuery cost control: capacity/slot commitments for predictable spend vs on-demand per-bytes-scanned. Partition + cluster to cut bytes scanned.
  • DMS = migrate to Cloud SQL/AlloyDB (minimal-downtime CDC). Datastream = ongoing CDC into BigQuery. Dataflow = managed Beam ETL. Cloud Composer = Airflow DAG orchestration.

Cloud Storage

ClassMin durationAccessUse case
StandardnoneHot / frequentWebsites, streaming, active data
Nearline30 days< monthlyBackups, monthly reports
Coldline90 days< quarterlyDR, infrequent archives
Archive365 days< yearlyLong-term/compliance archive
Trap: all classes have the same millisecond latency — colder is cheaper at rest / pricier to read, not slower. Deleting before the min duration still bills the full term. Use Autoclass when access is unknown.
  • "Auto-move to cheaper storage after N days"Lifecycle policy
  • "Immutable WORM compliance retention"Bucket Lock
  • "Temp access without a Google account"Signed URL
  • "PB-scale move, poor bandwidth"Transfer Appliance
  • "Online bulk from S3/Azure/on-prem"Storage Transfer Service
  • "Shared NFS file system across many VMs"Filestore
  • "Highest IOPS, OK to lose data (scratch)"Local SSD
  • "Disk-level HA across zones / stateful GKE"Regional PD
  • "Query Parquet in GCS without loading"BigLake / external tables
Domain · Networking

Networking

VPC structure, how you connect to on-prem, and which load balancer. The hybrid-connectivity ladder and the LB matrix are high-yield.

VPC fundamentals

  • VPC = global; subnets = regional. Custom mode for production. Secondary ranges = GKE Pods/Services (alias IPs).
  • Firewall rules: lower priority number wins (default 1000); implied deny-ingress + allow-egress; target by service account (preferred) or tag. Hierarchical policies apply org/folder-wide.
  • Shared VPC: central host project owns subnets/firewall, service projects deploy in. VPC Peering: two VPCs as equals, non-transitive. Network Connectivity Center: hub-and-spoke transitive connectivity.

Hybrid connectivity ladder

OptionBandwidthSLAUse when
HA VPN~3 Gbps/tunnel99.99%*Encrypted, low cost, quick (*redundant tunnels)
Partner Interconnect50 Mbps–50 Gbps99.9/99.99%Mid bandwidth, no colo presence
Dedicated Interconnect10–100 Gbps99.9/99.99%High bandwidth, private, has colo
Direct/Carrier Peeringup to 10 GbpsNoneReach only Google public APIs at edge
Trap: Interconnect is not encrypted by default. Need encryption → HA VPN over Interconnect / MACsec. Hybrid private routing with redundancy → Interconnect + Cloud Router (BGP). VPC Peering cannot reach on-prem.

Load balancing & private access

  • "Global HTTP(S), URL routing, CDN, WAF, anycast IP"Global Ext Application LB
  • "Preserve client source IP / UDP / non-TCP"Passthrough Network LB
  • "TLS offload, not HTTP, custom port, global"Proxy Network LB
  • "Balance between internal app tiers"Internal LB
  • "No-external-IP VM → reach Google APIs"Private Google Access
  • "Private VM outbound to internet only"Cloud NAT
  • "Private access to SaaS / published service"Private Service Connect

Global = Application LB & Proxy Network LB only; Passthrough Network LB is regional. Cloud CDN attaches to the External Application LB. Cloud DNS supports public + private zones and routing policies.

Domain · Security & IAM

Security & IAM

Least privilege everywhere, perimeters around data, the right key tier. Know who can do what (IAM) vs what may be configured (Org Policy) vs the data boundary (VPC-SC).

IAM & identity

  • Hierarchy Org → Folder → Project → Resource; policy inherited downward, the union of bindings. A child can't subtract a parent grant → use IAM Deny policy, Org Policy, or VPC-SC.
  • Roles: Basic (avoid in prod) → Predefined (preferred) → Custom. Assign to groups. IAM Conditions for attribute/time-based access.
  • Service accounts: attach to VM/GKE + metadata tokens (no key files). Workload Identity Federation = external/AWS/on-prem auth without keys. GKE Workload Identity = pod-scoped identity. Privileged Access Manager = just-in-time, time-bound elevation.

Keys, perimeters & defense

Encryption tier

  • "Control rotation, Google stores keys"CMEK (KMS)
  • "Google must never store the key"CSEK
  • "Keys fully outside Google"Cloud EKM
  • "FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware keys"Cloud HSM
  • "Store/rotate API keys & secrets"Secret Manager
  • "Google support needs my approval to access"Access Approval + Transparency

Access & data protection

  • "Block exfiltration w/ stolen creds"VPC Service Controls
  • "WAF: SQLi/XSS/DDoS, geo, rate limit"Cloud Armor
  • "Access internal app w/o VPN"IAP / BeyondCorp
  • "Central misconfig/threat view"Security Command Center
  • "Mask / tokenize / discover PII"Sensitive Data Protection
  • "Only signed/attested images deploy"Binary Authorization
Trap: Cloud Armor protects L7 web traffic at the edge — to stop data exfiltration even with valid credentials use VPC Service Controls. EU residency → Org Policy gcp.resourceLocations + Assured Workloads. Note: SCC Enterprise tier sunsets May 2027 → Premium.
Domain · Operations & Cost

Operations, Reliability & Cost

SRE vocabulary, disaster-recovery tiers by RPO/RTO, observability, and cost levers. These questions reward precise definitions.

SRE & observability

Term / toolMeaning
SLI / SLO / SLAMeasured metric / internal target / external contract (SLA looser than SLO)
Error budget100% − SLO. Exhausted → freeze risky releases (error-budget policy)
Multi-window, multi-burn-rate alertsPage early on fast burn, suppress noise — the right SLO alerting
Cloud TracePer-service latency spans to find the slow hop
Cloud LoggingLog Router → sinks to BigQuery/GCS/Pub-Sub for retention/analysis
Cloud Profiler / Error ReportingContinuous CPU/heap profiling / aggregated exceptions

Disaster recovery

RPO = acceptable data loss · RTO = acceptable downtime.

NeedStrategy
Hours OK, cheapestBackup & restore
Hours, low costCold standby (pilot light, rebuild from IaC)
Minutes (low RPO, ~15min RTO)Warm standby + continuous replication
Near-zero, any costHot / active-active multi-region

Single-zone-failure resilience without multi-region cost → regional MIG across zones + HA database.

Cost & IaC

  • "Steady 24/7 predictable"Committed Use Discount
  • "Automatic, no commitment"Sustained Use Discount
  • "Interruptible batch, cheapest"Spot VMs
  • "Alert on actual + forecasted spend"Budget + alerts
  • "Analyze cost / chargeback"Billing export → BigQuery + labels
  • "Reviewable, reproducible infra"Terraform / Infrastructure Manager
  • "Complex pipeline DAG w/ retries"Cloud Composer (Airflow)
Trap: Budget alerts notify only — they don't cap spend. Deployment Manager is end-of-support Mar 31 2026 → new IaC questions answer Terraform / Infrastructure Manager.
Domain · GenAI & ML (new in v6.1)

Generative AI & Vertex AI

New sections 2.4 (Vertex AI end-to-end ML) and 2.5 (pre-built APIs). Three of the four case studies are GenAI-centric, so this is high-yield. The core skill: pick the right model/API/service for the task.

Foundation models — when to pick each

ModelUse it when the scenario says…
GeminiSummarize/analyze/extract over multimodal content (text+image+video+audio+PDF); reasoning; build an agent / function-calling; code
ImagenGenerate or edit images — product/marketing visuals, inpainting, background removal
VeoGenerate video from text/image prompts
Chirp / Speech-to-TextTranscribe/understand speech at scale (use the dedicated API for pure transcription)
Key distinction: "generate an image/video" → Imagen/Veo. "Understand / summarize / extract / converse over media or docs" → Gemini.

Grounding, RAG & search

NeedService
Grounded search / Q&A over our documents (managed, ACL-aware) — default RAG answerVertex AI Search
Managed RAG but need a customizable pipelineVertex AI RAG Engine
Semantic / embedding similarity over millions of vectorsVertex AI Vector Search
Product search / recommendations for an online storeVertex AI Search for commerce (Discovery AI)

RAG pattern: ingest & chunk → embed → index in Vector Search → retrieve top-k → augment + ground → Gemini answers.

Conversational, safety & pre-built APIs

  • "Chatbot / replace the IVR / self-service agent"Conversational Agents (Dialogflow CX)
  • "Help live human agents on calls"Agent Assist
  • "Modernize the whole contact center"Contact Center AI
  • "Screen GenAI prompts/responses for safety"Model Armor
  • "Explain model decisions / regulatory"Explainable AI
  • "Detect model drift in production"Model Monitoring
  • "Extract fields from invoices / PDFs / forms"Document AI
  • "Sentiment / entities from text"Natural Language API
  • "ML on data already in BigQuery, via SQL"BigQuery ML
  • "End-to-end MLOps, online endpoints"Vertex AI (Pipelines/Registry/Endpoints)
Rule: a purpose-built API beats Gemini for standard structured tasks (transcribe, translate, OCR/extract, sentiment) — cheaper, faster, structured output. Gemini beats an API for cross-modal understanding, summarization/generation, reasoning, agents.
Cross-cutting · WAF & Migration

Well-Architected Framework & Migration

Both now examined. The WAF is the lens for "best balance" questions; migration covers the strategies, phases, and tooling.

The six WAF pillars

PillarQuestion signal → answer pattern
Operational Excellence"automate deploys / reduce toil" → IaC, CI/CD, managed services
Security, Privacy & Compliance"least privilege / protect data / HIPAA-GDPR / no exfiltration" → IAM, CMEK, DLP, VPC-SC
Reliability"must stay up / meet RTO-RPO / survive failure" → multi-zone/region, SLOs, tested DR
Cost Optimization"reduce/control spend" → autoscaling, rightsizing, CUDs/spot, budgets
Performance Optimization"slow / spikes / scale" → autoscaling, CDN/caching, right machine, load testing on real data
Sustainability"greener / lower carbon" → low-carbon regions, serverless, higher utilization

Migration: the Rs, phases & tools

StrategyTool / target
Rehost (lift & shift)Compute Engine via Migrate to Virtual Machines
Replatform (move & improve)DB → Cloud SQL/AlloyDB via DMS
Refactor (re-architect)Containerize → GKE/Cloud Run via Migrate to Containers; strangler-fig pattern
Repurchase / RetireSaaS replacement / decommission
Hybrid / multicloud one control planeGKE Enterprise (Anthos) + Config Management

Phases: Assess → Plan → Migrate → Optimize. Migration Center (now required) does discovery, dependency mapping, TCO. Prefer phased waves over big-bang. Huge data + slow link → Transfer Appliance (offline); good link → Storage Transfer Service (online).

Design · Reference Architectures

Reference Architectures & Diagrams

The exam rewards recognizing the shape of a solution. These are the canonical GCP patterns — learn each diagram and the "why" so you can assemble the right one under any case study. Boxes are color-coded by domain.

1 · Global, highly available web application

Usersworldwide
↓
Cloud DNSglobal resolution
↓
Cloud ArmorWAF · DDoS · rate limit
Global External Application LBsingle anycast IP · TLS
Cloud CDNedge caching
↓
Regional MIG — Region Aautoscaling · multi-zone
Regional MIG — Region Bautoscaling · multi-zone
↓
Cloud SQL (HA)regional failover + replicas
Memorystorecache, sub-ms reads

Why: one anycast IP at the Global External Application LB gives global routing, TLS offload, and native Cloud Armor + Cloud CDN. Regional MIGs across zones survive a zone failure; Cloud SQL HA gives an automatic standby. Trigger: "global web app, single IP, URL routing, protect from attacks."

2 · Microservices on GKE with CI/CD

Clients
Cloud Build → Artifact Registry → Cloud DeployCI/CD · Binary Authorization
↓
Global External Application LBIngress
↓
GKE regional cluster (Autopilot) · Cloud Service Mesh
Service AHPA
Service BHPA
Service CHPA
↓
Spanner / Cloud SQLstate
Pub/Subasync messaging
Workload Identitykeyless SA

Why: GKE Autopilot runs many services with per-pod billing and no node management; HPA scales on load (custom/external metrics for RPS). Pub/Sub decouples services; Workload Identity gives keyless API access. Trigger: "microservices, portability, service mesh, scale independently."

3 · Streaming + batch data analytics pipeline

IoT / app eventsstream
On-prem / filesbatch
↓
Pub/Subingest, decouple
Cloud Storagedata lake (+ BigLake)
↓
Dataflowmanaged Beam · stream + batch ETL
↓
BigQuerywarehouse · analytics · BQML
Bigtablelow-latency key lookups
↓
Looker / BIdashboards

Why: Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery is the canonical analytics chain; Bigtable serves real-time per-key reads in parallel. Trigger: "ingest high-volume events, real-time + historical analytics, predictive maintenance." (KnightMotives / TerramEarth-style.)

4 · Hybrid connectivity with Shared VPC

On-prem data center+ Active Directory
↓   Cloud Router (BGP)   ↓
Dedicated Interconnectprivate, 10–100 Gbps, SLA
↓
Shared VPC — host project owns subnets & firewall
Service project A
Service project B
Private Google Accessreach APIs, no external IP

Why: Dedicated Interconnect gives private high-bandwidth on-prem connectivity (add HA VPN/MACsec if encryption required — Interconnect is not encrypted by default). Shared VPC centralizes the network while teams deploy in service projects. Trigger: "central network team, hybrid, secure high-performance link."

5 · Generative AI — RAG (grounded answers over your data)

Enterprise docs
→ chunk →
Embeddings model
→
Vector Searchindex
— — — query time — — —
User query
→
Model Armorprompt safety
→
Retrieve top-kfrom Vector Search
↓
Geminigrounded generation
→
Model Armorresponse safety
→
Cited answer

Why: grounding stops hallucination by retrieving real context before generation. Managed shortcut: Vertex AI Search does ingest + retrieve + ground + cite out of the box — pick it unless you need a custom pipeline (then Vector Search / RAG Engine). Model Armor screens prompts and responses. Trigger: "grounded Q&A / chatbot over our documents." (Altostrat / Cymbal-style.)

6 · Multi-region disaster recovery (active-active)

Users
↓
Global External Application LBhealth-check failover
↓    both regions serve live    ↓
Region A — full stacklive traffic
Region B — full stacklive traffic
↓
Spanner (multi-region)strong consistency · 99.999%

Why: near-zero RPO/RTO needs both regions live with a globally consistent datastore (Spanner). Cheaper tiers: warm standby (minutes), cold standby / backup-restore (hours). Match the tier to the stated RPO/RTO and budget. Trigger: "survive a regional outage, near-zero data loss."

7 · Secure landing zone & governance

OrganizationOrg Policies inherited down
↓
Folder: Prod
Folder: Non-prod
Folder: Shared
↓
VPC Service Controls perimeter (anti-exfiltration)
Project: app
Project: data (BigQuery, GCS) + CMEK
Project: loggingsink → BigQuery

Why: hierarchy + Org Policies enforce guardrails (no external IPs, EU-only regions); VPC-SC blocks data exfiltration even with stolen credentials; a central log sink and CMEK satisfy audit/compliance. Trigger: "enterprise governance, residency, least privilege, prevent exfiltration."

8 · EHR Healthcare — end-to-end (case study)

On-prem AD + legacy
↔ Interconnect ↔
Cloud Identity + GCDSSSO federation
↓
Cloud Armor
Global External Application LB
↓
Regional GKE Enterprise (Anthos) fleet · 99.9% HA
EHR servicescontainers
Cloud SQL (HA) · Memorystore · Atlas
↓
BigQuery + BQMLtrend insights
Cloud Monitoring + Loggingcentralized
VPC-SC + CMEK + BAAHIPAA

Why: this assembles the EHR case study — hybrid identity from existing AD, regional GKE for 99.9%, managed data tier, BigQuery for insights, centralized observability, and a HIPAA-aligned security perimeter. Study how each requirement maps to a box.

Serverless Event-Driven Pipeline

Client / IoT DeviceEvent producer
GCS UploadObject trigger
↓
EventarcEvent routing & filtering
Pub/SubMessage queue & fan-out
↓
Cloud RunStateless container processing (scales to 0)
↓
FirestoreTransactional / operational store
BigQueryAnalytics & long-term storage

Why: Fully serverless; no VMs to manage. Eventarc routes GCP-native events (GCS, Pub/Sub, Audit Logs) to Cloud Run without custom polling logic. Cloud Run scales to zero between bursts. Firestore handles low-latency reads; BigQuery handles analytical queries over the full dataset.

Internal 3-Tier App with Internal Application LB

Internal ClientsEmployees / services within VPC
↓
Internal Application LBL7 HTTP/S routing, path-based rules, regional
↓
Application Tier (Private Subnet)
GKE Autopilot / Cloud RunMicroservices (no public IP)
Compute Engine MIGStateful app servers
↓
Data Tier (Private Subnet)
Cloud SQL (HA)Relational DB — primary + standby
MemorystoreSession cache (Redis)
↓
Cloud NATOutbound internet for patching (no inbound)
VPC Firewall / NGFWLeast-privilege ingress/egress rules

Why: Internal Application LB keeps traffic private — no public IPs on any tier. Path-based routing lets a single LB front multiple backend services. Cloud SQL HA provides 99.95% SLA with automatic failover. Cloud NAT allows egress without exposing instances. This pattern meets compliance requirements for internet-facing apps that must keep backend infrastructure private.

IoT / Edge with Google Distributed Cloud + Pub/Sub + Bigtable

Edge (Factory / Remote Site)
IoT Sensors / PLCsRaw telemetry at the edge
↓
Google Distributed Cloud (Hosted)On-prem Kubernetes; local inference + buffering
↓ (HTTPS / MQTT)
Google Cloud (Region)
Pub/SubIngest telemetry at scale; decouple producers
↓
Dataflow (Apache Beam)Stream processing, windowing, enrichment
↓
Cloud BigtableTime-series store; millisecond reads; petabyte scale
BigQueryHistorical analytics & dashboards
↓
Vertex AIPredictive maintenance & anomaly detection models

Why: Google Distributed Cloud runs Kubernetes at the edge for local processing and resilience (continues if WAN is down). Pub/Sub absorbs bursty sensor data without back-pressure. Bigtable's row-key design (device-id + reverse-timestamp) gives O(1) lookups for latest readings. Dataflow handles late-arriving events with watermarks. Vertex AI closes the loop with model inference pushed back to edge via GDC.

Secure Data Warehouse: VPC-SC + CMEK + BigLake

VPC Service Controls Perimeter
VPC Service ControlsAPI-level perimeter; blocks exfiltration to external identities
↓ (only perimeter members can access)
Cloud Storage (Data Lake)Raw + curated data; CMEK-encrypted buckets
BigQueryDWH tables; CMEK on datasets; column-level security
↓
BigLakeUnified engine; query GCS + BQ with consistent policy
↓
Cloud DLP (SDP)Scan, de-identify PII before sharing
DataplexData governance, lineage, catalog
↓ (governed access only)
Cloud KMS / HSMCMEK key management; customer controls rotation
Vertex AI WorkbenchNotebooks inside perimeter; no data leaves

Why: VPC Service Controls wrap the entire data tier — even a compromised credential cannot exfiltrate data to an external project. CMEK ensures Google cannot decrypt data without customer-held keys (compliance: HIPAA, PCI). BigLake enforces a single fine-grained access policy across GCS and BigQuery, preventing policy drift. Cloud DLP automatically redacts PII before data leaves the curated zone. Dataplex provides audit lineage required for regulatory reporting.

Architecture design checklist (apply to any scenario)

  • Availability: zonal → regional → multi-region. Add a regional MIG + Global LB for customer-facing SLAs.
  • Right datastore: walk the DB matrix — relational/NoSQL, regional/global, OLTP/OLAP.
  • Decouple: Pub/Sub between producers and consumers; Dataflow for transform.
  • Least privilege + perimeter: predefined roles, Workload Identity, VPC-SC, CMEK.
  • Connectivity: Interconnect for high-bandwidth private; HA VPN for quick/encrypted; Shared VPC for central control.
  • Cost & ops: autoscaling, CUDs for steady load, Spot for batch, IaC (Terraform), budgets + monitoring.
  • Match the executive priority and don't over-engineer.
Case Study · 1 of 4

EHR Healthcare

Priority signal: operational consistency & reliability. SaaS electronic-health-records provider moving off expiring colo data centers; apps already containerized. Data in MySQL, MS SQL Server, Redis, MongoDB.

📄 Read the full official case study

Official source PDF

Company Overview

EHR Healthcare is a leading provider of electronic health record software to the medical industry. EHR Healthcare provides their software as a service to multi-national medical offices, hospitals, and insurance providers.

Solution Concept

Due to rapid changes in the healthcare and insurance industry, EHR Healthcare's business has been growing exponentially year over year. They need to be able to scale their environment, adapt their disaster recovery plan, and roll out new continuous deployment capabilities to update their software at a fast pace. Google Cloud has been chosen to replace their current colocation facilities.

Existing Technical Environment

  • EHR's software is currently hosted in multiple colocation facilities. The lease on one of the data centers is about to expire.
  • Customer-facing applications are web-based, and many have recently been containerized to run on a group of Kubernetes clusters. Data is stored in a mixture of relational and NoSQL databases (MySQL, MS SQL Server, Redis, and MongoDB).
  • EHR is hosting several legacy file- and API-based integrations with insurance providers on-premises. There are no current plans to upgrade or migrate these legacy systems.
  • Users are managed via Microsoft Active Directory. Monitoring is currently being done via various open source tools. Alerts and notifications are sent via email and are often ignored.

Business Requirements

  • On-board new insurance providers as quickly as possible.
  • Provide a minimum 99.9% availability for all customer-facing systems.
  • Provide centralized visibility and proactive action on system performance and usage.
  • Increase ability to provide insights into healthcare trends.
  • Reduce latency to all customers.
  • Maintain regulatory compliance.
  • Decrease infrastructure administration costs.
  • Make predictions and generate reports on industry trends based on provider data.

Technical Requirements

  • Maintain legacy interfaces to insurance providers with connectivity to both on-premises systems and cloud providers.
  • Provide a consistent way to manage customer-facing applications that are container-based.
  • Provide a secure and high-performance connection between on-premises systems and Google Cloud.
  • Provide consistent logging, log retention, monitoring, and alerting capabilities.
  • Maintain and manage multiple container-based environments.
  • Dynamically scale and provision new environments.
  • Create interfaces to ingest and process data from new providers.

Executive Statement

Our on-premises strategy has worked for years but has required a major investment of time and money in training our team on distinctly different systems, managing similar but separate environments, and responding to outages. Many of these outages have been a result of misconfigured systems, inadequate capacity to manage spikes in traffic, and inconsistent monitoring practices. We want to use Google Cloud to leverage a scalable, resilient platform that can span multiple environments seamlessly and provide a consistent and stable user experience that positions us for future growth.

Requirements → architecture

RequirementServiceWhy
99.9% availability for containerized appsRegional GKE + Global Ext App LBMulti-zone redundancy + anycast LB w/ health checks
Consistent mgmt across on-prem + cloudGKE Enterprise (Anthos) + Config SyncSingle config/policy plane across hybrid clusters
Secure high-performance on-prem linkDedicated InterconnectHigh throughput, low latency, private (HA VPN as backup)
Relational data (MySQL, MS SQL)Cloud SQL (regional HA)Managed, auto failover, cuts DB admin
Redis / MongoDBMemorystore / MongoDB AtlasManaged cache / Atlas on Marketplace
Existing Microsoft Active DirectoryCloud Identity + GCDS / SSOReuse AD as source of truth
Centralized logging, monitoring, alertingCloud Monitoring + LoggingReplaces ignored email alerts; proactive dashboards
Healthcare trend insights & predictionsBigQuery + BigQuery MLServerless analytics + ML on provider data
HIPAA complianceBAA + CMEK + VPC-SC + least-privilege IAMProtect PHI

Likely exam questions — tap to reveal

Minimum 99.9% availability for apps already running in GKE — best approach?
Regional GKE cluster behind a Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer. Regional = multi-zone redundancy; a zonal cluster can't meet the SLA.
Secure, high-bandwidth, low-latency link to keep legacy insurance integrations reachable?
Dedicated Interconnect. Highest-performance private connectivity; HA VPN can't match sustained throughput/latency.
Alerts are emailed and ignored; they want centralized proactive visibility — implement what?
Cloud Monitoring dashboards + alerting policies with Cloud Logging. Native, centralized, proactive.
Existing users are in Microsoft Active Directory — manage GCP identities with least effort?
Google Cloud Directory Sync into Cloud Identity, federate SSO. Reuse the existing source of truth instead of recreating users.
Manage container workloads consistently across on-prem and Google Cloud?
GKE Enterprise (Anthos) fleet with Config Management. One consistent config/policy plane across hybrid.
Migrate many on-prem VMware VMs with minimal downtime?
Migrate to Virtual Machines (M2VM). Automated replication + low-downtime cutover; DMS is for databases only.
Case Study · 2 of 4

Altostrat Media

Priority signal: reliability + cost first, then GenAI. Large audio/video library already on GCP (GKE, Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Cloud Run). Wants GenAI for summaries, metadata, recommendations, harmful-content detection — but the exec ranks reliability and cost control above all.

📄 Read the full official case study

Official source PDF

Company Overview

Altostrat is a prominent player in the media industry, with an extensive collection of audio and video content that comprises podcasts, interviews, news broadcasts, and documentaries. Their success in delivering premium content to a diverse audience requires a content management system that can keep pace with the dynamic media landscape.

Solution Concept

Altostrat seeks to modernize its content management and user engagement strategies using Google Cloud's generative AI. They want a platform that empowers customers with personalized recommendations, natural language interactions, and seamless self-service support. Simultaneously, they want to drive revenue growth through dynamic pricing, targeted marketing, and personalized product suggestions. The seamless integration of AI-powered tools into the existing Google Cloud environment will enable Altostrat to efficiently manage their vast media library, enhance user experiences, and unlock new revenue streams.

Existing Technical Environment

  • Altostrat's content management and delivery platform leverages GKE for scalability and high availability, essential for handling their vast media library.
  • Their extensive media library spanning various documents, audio and video formats is stored in Cloud Storage.
  • To gain valuable insights into user behavior, content consumption patterns, and audience demographics, Altostrat leverages BigQuery as their primary data warehouse.
  • Additionally, they use Cloud Run functions for serverless execution of event-driven tasks such as video transcoding, metadata extraction, and personalized content recommendations.
  • While Altostrat has made significant strides in cloud adoption, they also maintain some legacy on-premises systems for specific workflows like content ingestion and archival.

Business Requirements

  • Inform content strategy and decision making with data.
  • Accelerate and enhance the reliability of operational workflows across all environments.
  • Simplify infrastructure management for rapid application deployment.
  • Optimize cloud storage costs while maintaining high availability and scalability for media content.
  • Enable natural language interaction with the platform with 24/7 user support.
  • Automatically generate concise summaries of media content.

Technical Requirements

  • Modernize CI/CD for containerized deployments with a centralized management platform.
  • Secure, high-performance hybrid cloud connectivity for data ingestion.
  • Provide scalable, performant Kubernetes environments both on-premises and in the cloud.
  • Optimize cloud storage costs for growing media volumes.
  • Design AI-powered detection of harmful content.
  • Ensure that AI systems are auditable and their decisions can be explained.
  • Leverage LLMs and conversational AI for personalized experiences and content virality.
  • Develop advanced chatbots with natural language understanding to provide personalized assistance.
  • Automated summarization for diverse media.

Executive Statement

At Altostrat, we are embracing the next frontier of artificial intelligence to revolutionize our content strategy. The seamless integration of AI-powered tools into the existing Google Cloud environment will enable Altostrat to efficiently manage their vast media library, enhance user experiences, and unlock new revenue streams. Google Cloud's generative AI will solidify their leadership in the media industry.

Requirements → architecture

RequirementServiceWhy
Optimize storage cost for a growing libraryCloud Storage AutoclassAuto-tiers by access pattern — the cost lever the exec names
Summaries of media; multimodal summarizationVertex AI + GeminiSummarizes audio/video/text natively; managed = reliable
Rich metadata via NLP + computer visionVideo Intelligence + Vision + Speech-to-TextPurpose-built media understanding
Detect & filter inappropriate contentVideo Intel / Vision SafeSearch + Model ArmorManaged safety at content + model layers
24/7 natural-language supportConversational Agents (Dialogflow CX)Managed NLU agent grounded on content
Personalized recommendationsVertex AI + Vector SearchSemantic similarity over the catalog
AI must be auditable / explainableVertex Explainable AI + Audit LogsFeature attributions + lineage + audit trail
Elastic, cost-effective media processingCloud Run / GKE + Pub/Sub (Transcoder API)Scales to zero = cost-efficient vs always-on VMs

Likely exam questions — tap to reveal

Storage cost is a top exec priority and the library keeps growing — cut cost without manual tiering?
Enable Autoclass on the buckets. Auto-moves objects between tiers by access; no lifecycle tuning.
Auto-generate concise summaries of podcasts and video — most appropriate managed service?
Vertex AI using Gemini (multimodal). Summarizes multimodal media via a managed model.
Detect and block inappropriate content and keep GenAI outputs safe?
Video Intelligence / Vision explicit-content detection + Model Armor on prompts/responses. Managed safety at both layers.
24/7 natural-language support chatbot grounded in their content?
Vertex AI Agent Builder / Conversational Agents (Dialogflow CX). Managed conversational AI with NLU + grounding.
Auditors require recommendation decisions be explainable — which capabilities?
Vertex Explainable AI + Model Registry/Monitoring with Cloud Audit Logs. Attributions + lineage + audit.
Process large volumes of uploaded video elastically and cost-effectively?
Cloud Storage for media + Cloud Run/GKE for processing + Vertex AI for analysis. Always-on peak-sized VMs waste cost; Cloud SQL can't store large blobs.
Case Study · 3 of 4

Cymbal Retail

Priority signal: cost + applied GenAI + human-in-the-loop. Fast-growing online retailer with a huge catalog. Wants GenAI catalog enrichment, conversational commerce, and a modernized stack — with a required staff approval gate before AI content goes live.

📄 Read the full official case study

Official source PDF

Company Overview

Cymbal is an online retailer experiencing significant growth. The retailer specializes in a large assortment of products spanning several retail sub-verticals, which makes managing their extensive product catalog a constant challenge.

Solution Concept

Cymbal wants to modernize its operations and enhance the customer experience in three core areas:

  • Catalog and Content Enrichment: Cymbal wants to automate and improve the accuracy of their product catalog by utilizing Gen AI to generate product attributes, descriptions, and images from supplier-provided information. This solution will streamline their catalog management, reduce manual effort and errors, and ensure information is consistent across all their sales channels.
  • Conversational Commerce with Product Discovery: Cymbal wants to implement a Conversational Commerce solution by integrating AI-powered virtual agents into their website and mobile app to provide customers with a personalized and intuitive shopping experience through natural language conversations. These agents will utilize Google Cloud's Discovery AI to process user requests and retrieve the most relevant products based on each customer's needs and preferences.
  • Technical Stack Modernization: To streamline operations and reduce costs around manual processes, data transfer, error handling and remediation, Cymbal wants to modernize their technical stack with cloud-based infrastructure, secure and efficient data handling, 3rd party integrations, and proactive monitoring and security.

Existing Technical Environment

  • Cymbal currently relies on a mix of on-premises and cloud-based systems with a variety of databases, including MySQL, Microsoft SQL Server, Redis, and MongoDB, to store and manage its vast product catalog and customer data.
  • A custom-built web application allows customers to browse the product catalog by querying the relational databases for names and categories of products.
  • An IVR (Interactive Voice Response) system handles initial customer calls and routes them to appropriate departments or agents.
  • Call center agents receive transferred calls from the IVR system and manually enter orders into the system when a customer cannot complete a transaction on their own.
  • Various open source tools for monitoring such as Grafana, Nagios, and Elastic are in use.
  • The current technical environment has encountered significant challenges: manual processes are time-consuming and error-prone, data silos limit a unified view of the customer journey, and integrating new technologies is difficult.

Business Requirements

  • Automate Product Catalog Enrichment: Reduce manual effort, minimize errors, and ensure accuracy and consistency across the product catalog.
  • Drive Sales Conversion: Provide a more intuitive and helpful shopping experience to improve sales conversion rates and drive revenue growth.
  • Reduce costs: Reduce call center staffing costs and data-center hosting costs.

Technical Requirements

  • Attribute Generation: Accurately derive relevant product attributes from various supplier data, including titles, descriptions, and images, ensuring the attributes align with the product category and Cymbal's existing catalog structure.
  • Image Generation and Enhancement: Generate different product image variations from a base image (e.g., showcasing various colors). It should also support background changes, product color adjustments, and the addition of text overlays.
  • Automate Product Discovery: Process customer requests expressed in natural language and return highly relevant product results.

Executive Statement

By implementing Google Cloud's Gen AI for Digital Commerce solutions, Cymbal can transform its online retail operations to improve efficiency, enhance customer experience, and drive revenue growth.

Requirements → architecture

RequirementServiceWhy
Generate attributes/descriptions from supplier text+imagesVertex AI + Gemini (multimodal)Reads images + text → structured attributes
Generate / enhance product imagesVertex AI ImagenImage generation/editing — exactly this use case
Natural-language product discoveryVertex AI Search for commerce + Dialogflow CXRetail-tuned discovery + NL virtual agent
Staff review before publishingHuman-in-the-Loop approval workflowBrief explicitly requires approve/reject/modify gate
Reduce call-center staffingConversational Agents + Agent Assist (CCAI)Automates/deflects calls
Modernize databases (MySQL/MS SQL/Mongo/Redis)Cloud SQL / Atlas / MemorystoreManaged; cuts data-center hosting cost
Real-time personalization + consistent inventoryVertex AI + Memorystore + SpannerPersonalization + spike caching + globally consistent inventory
Protect customer/agent dataDLP + CMEK + VPC-SC + IAMDetect/protect PII, prevent exfiltration

Likely exam questions — tap to reveal

Auto-generate product descriptions and attributes from supplier images and text?
Vertex AI with Gemini (multimodal). One model ingests images+text → structured output.
Create product image variations (colors, backgrounds, overlays) from a base image?
Vertex AI Imagen. Purpose-built image generation/editing; other services can't generate images.
Natural-language product discovery and AI virtual agents on web/mobile?
Vertex AI Search for commerce (Discovery AI) + Conversational Agents. Retail-tuned discovery + managed NL agent.
GenAI catalog content must be reviewed by staff before going live — design?
A Human-in-the-Loop approval workflow that gates publishing. The brief explicitly requires associate approve/reject/modify.
Real-time personalized recommendations during flash sales with consistent inventory?
Vertex AI (recs) + Memorystore (spike caching) + Spanner (global inventory). A single regional Cloud SQL can't meet flash-sale scale or global consistency.
Reduce call-center staffing from IVR + manual order entry?
Conversational Agents (Dialogflow CX) with Contact Center AI / Agent Assist. Automates and deflects calls.
Case Study · 4 of 4

KnightMotives Automotive

Priority signal: safety + data at scale + security/GDPR. Autonomous-vehicle maker; massive telemetry, legacy mainframe/ERP, past data breaches, hard EU/GDPR residency requirement, poor rural connectivity. Wants a consistent AI in-vehicle experience and data monetization.

📄 Read the full official case study

Official source PDF

Company Overview

KnightMotives is a global automotive manufacturer focused on modernizing the consumer experience across all vehicle types by leveraging AI and improving their online ordering system. KnightMotives provides connected-car services and gathers telemetry from vehicles to improve maintenance and driver services, and shares selective data with partners such as insurers and dealerships.

Solution Concept

KnightMotives wants to shift from manufacturing cars to creating a complete and compelling "automotive experience." Their strategy prioritizes delivering a consistent experience across all models, developing AI-powered features, generating new revenue from data monetization, adopting a digital focus to differentiate their brand from competitors, and developing better tools for mechanics and salespeople. Google Cloud has been selected to help KnightMotives modernize their IT infrastructure and enable real-time data capabilities at global scale.

Existing Technical Environment

  • KnightMotives's IT is largely on-premises with some applications on major cloud platforms.
  • Their supply chain runs on an outdated mainframe, and Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) is also outdated, making new promotions and dealer discounts difficult to implement.
  • Dealers have no budget for new equipment.
  • There is fragmentation across vehicles with multiple code bases, and significant technical debt from supporting backwards compatibility.
  • Network connectivity to manufacturing plants and vehicle connectivity in rural areas are ongoing challenges.
  • Security is a paramount concern due to past data breaches.
  • Adherence to European Union (EU) data protection regulations, especially for emerging autonomous platforms, is critical.
  • Vehicle data is currently batch-uploaded from vehicles to an on-premises data center, which limits scalability and real-time analysis.

Business Requirements

  • Foster a personalized relationship with the driver and deliver a cohesive experience across all KnightMotives vehicle models.
  • Improve the online build-to-order system to provide a delightful experience for dealers and customers.
  • Develop modern dealer tools to streamline dealer operations, including sales, service, and inventory management.
  • Implement a comprehensive Customer Relationship Management (CRM) system to track customer interactions, personalize experiences, and improve customer satisfaction.
  • Generate new revenue streams through data monetization while maintaining data privacy and regulatory compliance.
  • Differentiate the KnightMotives brand from competitors through a digital-first approach and AI-powered features.

Technical Requirements

  • Modernize the in-vehicle experience by developing a consistent user experience (UX) that seamlessly integrates AI-powered features across all models.
  • Update in-vehicle hardware and software in legacy models to support new UX features and AI capabilities.
  • Ensure reliable network connectivity, especially in rural areas, to support real-time AI features and data transmission.
  • Provide scalable, real-time ingestion of vehicle telemetry data (location, sensor readings, etc.) at global scale, replacing the current batch-upload approach.
  • Respect data locality by keeping regional data in-region to meet EU data protection regulations and other applicable compliance requirements.
  • Expose data to partners (insurers, dealerships) via secure APIs without compromising security.
  • Modernize the outdated ERP and supply chain systems to support dynamic pricing, new promotions, and dealer discount management.
  • Implement comprehensive security controls and monitoring to address past data breach vulnerabilities.

Executive Statement

KnightMotives is committed to enhancing safety and saving lives by leveraging an extensive body of data — encompassing driving, road conditions, behavioral studies, and crash safety statistics — to create compelling digital experiences for drivers. Our AI consistently outperforms national safety statistics, ensuring the unique and coveted KnightMotives experience is aligned across all our vehicle models. — Michael Knight, KnightMotives CEO

Requirements → architecture

RequirementServiceWhy
Ingest massive real-time fleet telemetryPub/Sub → DataflowGlobal scalable streaming ingestion + transform
Real-time lookups + analytics on telemetryBigtable + BigQueryBigtable for low-latency keys; BigQuery for analytics
AI inference in rural / poor-connectivity vehiclesGoogle Distributed Cloud (edge) + GKELow-latency inference; tolerates intermittent links
Hybrid; modernize legacy graduallyGKE Enterprise (Anthos) + Migrate to ContainersConsistent hybrid platform, incremental modernization
Keep EU data in EU (GDPR)Org Policy resourceLocations + Assured WorkloadsEnforce residency centrally
Post-breach threat posture & IRSecurity Command Center + Google SecOpsCentralized posture, detection, incident response
Monetize governed datasets with partnersBigQuery Analytics HubShare/monetize curated data without copying
AV model training & simulationVertex AI + GPUs/TPUs (Batch)Managed scalable ML + parallel simulation
Unreliable dealer build-to-order APIsApigee API ManagementStandardize, secure, throttle, monitor APIs

Likely exam questions — tap to reveal

Ingest high-velocity telemetry from a global vehicle fleet — best ingestion service?
Pub/Sub (then Dataflow for processing). Globally scalable, decoupled, managed streaming front door.
Real-time AI inference in vehicles with poor rural connectivity?
Google Distributed Cloud (edge) running GKE, syncing when connected. Consistent low-latency inference at the edge.
EU regulations require vehicle data stay in the EU — enforce across the org?
Org Policy resource-location constraint pinning resources to EU regions (+ Assured Workloads). Central residency enforcement.
After past breaches, want centralized threat detection & posture management?
Security Command Center (Premium/Enterprise). Org-wide posture, vulnerability, and threat management.
Monetize governed corporate datasets with partners/dealers?
BigQuery Analytics Hub. Securely publish/share/monetize curated datasets without copying data.
End-to-end telemetry platform for real-time alerts, analytics, and secure sharing?
Pub/Sub + Dataflow + Bigtable + BigQuery + Analytics Hub. A single Cloud SQL can't absorb the throughput or scale for analytics.
GCP PCA v6.1 · Reference

Numbers & Limits to Memorize

Hard facts the exam tests directly. If you have to guess, these are the numbers that will save you.

Cloud Storage — Classes & Minimum Durations

Storage Class Min Duration Retrieval Fee Use Case Key Exam Trap
StandardNoneNoneFrequent / hot dataMost expensive per GB; no retrieval cost
Nearline30 days~$0.01/GBOnce a month or lessAccessed weekly? → Standard, not Nearline
Coldline90 days~$0.02/GBOnce a quarter90-day minimum; early delete still charged
Archive365 days~$0.05/GBYearly / disaster recoveryNo real-time SLA; highest retrieval cost
Trap: Autoclass buckets waive retrieval fees — but Autoclass manages transitions automatically. Manual lifecycle rules still trigger early-deletion charges.

SLAs — Availability Guarantees

Service / Config SLA Notes
Cloud Spanner — Multi-Region99.999%Enterprise Plus edition; 5+ replicas across regions
Cloud Spanner — Regional99.99%Enterprise edition; 3 replicas in different zones
Cloud SQL — Enterprise Plus (HA)99.99%Near-zero downtime maintenance; replication-based HA
Cloud SQL — Enterprise (HA)99.95%Standby in same zone group; failover ~60 s
GKE — Regional cluster control plane99.95%Master spread across 3 zones
GKE — Zonal cluster control plane99.5%Single-zone master; avoid for production
HA VPN (properly configured)99.99%Two gateways, 4 tunnels; BGP required
Classic VPN99.9%Single gateway, single tunnel; lower availability
Dedicated Interconnect — 99.99% config99.99%≥4 connections across ≥2 metros in ≥2 edge domains
Dedicated Interconnect — 99.9% config99.9%≥2 connections, same metro, different edge domains
Partner Interconnect — 99.99% config99.99%≥4 VLAN attachments across 2 regions
Cloud DNS100%Anycast; financially backed 100% uptime
Cloud Load Balancing (Global)99.99%Global Anycast; no pre-warming needed

Cloud Spanner — Sizing

Unit Equiv. Storage Allotment Notes
100 Processing Units (PUs)0.1 node~1 TiB per 100 PUsMinimum granularity below 1 node
1,000 Processing Units1 node10 TiB per nodeAt ≥1 node, specify in whole nodes or multiples of 1000 PUs
Multi-region node1 node10 TiB per nodeBacked by 5+ replicas; higher cost than regional
Note: Storage was doubled from 2 TiB → 4 TiB per node, then again to 10 TiB. For the exam, the current guidance is 10 TiB/node for instances ≥1 node. Always size PUs/nodes for compute; Spanner storage scales separately up to the allotment.

Serverless Timeouts & Concurrency

Service Max Request Timeout Max Concurrency / Instances Key Fact
Cloud Run (Services)60 min (3,600 s)1,000 concurrent requests / instanceDefault timeout 5 min; default concurrency 80 × vCPUs
Cloud Run (Jobs)168 hours per task (7 days)10,000 tasks per jobNo HTTP; batch / long-running workloads
Cloud Functions Gen 1 (HTTP)9 min (540 s)1 concurrent request / instanceHard ceiling — cannot be extended
Cloud Functions Gen 1 (Background)9 min (540 s)1 per instanceSame limit regardless of trigger type
Cloud Functions Gen 2 (HTTP)60 min (3,600 s)1,000 (built on Cloud Run)Runs on Cloud Run; inherits concurrency support
Cloud Functions Gen 2 (Event-driven)9 min (540 s)1,000 per functionEvent-driven still capped at 9 min even in gen2
App Engine Standard — Auto scaling10 min (600 s)Scales to 0; fast cold startCannot run background threads
App Engine Standard — Manual/Basic24 hoursStays warm; persistent connectionsGood for WebSockets, long-running tasks
App Engine Flexible60 minAlways on; no scale-to-zeroDocker-based; slower cold start than Standard
Trap: Cloud Functions Gen 2 event-driven functions are still limited to 9 minutes — only HTTP-triggered Gen 2 functions get the full 60-minute window.

VPC Firewall Rules

Property Value Notes
Priority range0 – 65535Lower number = higher priority; 0 is highest
Default priority1000Applied when no priority is specified
Implied deny-all ingressPriority 65535Exists in every VPC; cannot be deleted
Implied allow-all egressPriority 65535Exists in every VPC; cannot be deleted
Default network allow-sshPriority 65534Only in the auto-created "default" VPC
DirectionINGRESS or EGRESSEach rule applies to one direction only
StatefulYesReturn traffic automatically allowed
Hierarchical firewallOrg / Folder levelOverrides VPC rules; evaluated first

IAM Hierarchy

Level Scope Overrides? Exam Tip
OrganizationAll projects/foldersYes — cannot be denied by lower levelsAssign broad roles here sparingly
FolderProjects under folderAdds to org-level; can't reduceUse for BU/team boundaries
ProjectResources in projectAdds to folder; can't reduceMost common grant level
ResourceSingle resourceAdds to project; can't reduceFine-grained; e.g., specific GCS bucket
Trap: IAM is additive — granting Owner at the project level cannot be overridden by a Viewer grant on a resource. The most permissive binding wins across the hierarchy.

Committed Use Discounts (CUDs)

Type 1-Year Discount 3-Year Discount Notes
Compute Engine — Resource-based (vCPU/RAM)~37%~55%Tied to specific region + machine family
Compute Engine — Spend-based (Flexible)~28%~46%Applies across billing account; more flexible
Memory-optimized VMs (M2/M3 series)up to 70%up to 70%Highest discount tier
Cloud SQL — Resource-based~25%~52%Per instance / region commitment
Cloud Spanner — Spend-basedVariesVariesUse Spanner editions pricing model
Note: Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs) are automatic (no commitment) for N1 and memory-optimized (M) VMs — up to 30% for full month; N2, N2D, C2 cap at 20%. SUDs and CUDs do not stack.

RPO / RTO Tiers

Tier RPO RTO Typical Architecture
Hot / Active-Active~0 (near-zero)SecondsMulti-region Spanner, Global LB, active replicas
Warm StandbyMinutesMinutesCloud SQL HA, GKE regional + standby region
Pilot LightHoursHoursMinimal infra in DR region; scale up on event
Cold Backup / Backup & RestoreHours–daysHours–daysCloud Storage snapshots, GCS exports, Coldline

Common VPC & Networking Limits

Resource Default / Max Notes
VPC networks per project15 (default) / requestableAuto-mode creates one subnet per region automatically
Subnets per VPCUnlimited (practical ~15,000)Each subnet is regional; can expand CIDR in place
VPC peering per network25 (default)Peering is non-transitive; no overlapping CIDRs
Shared VPC — host projects1 host per service projectService projects can access host VPC resources
Cloud Router — BGP sessionsUnlimited (practical 250 per router)Required for HA VPN and Cloud Interconnect dynamic routing
External IPs (global)Ephemeral or static; limited by quotaStatic IPs billed when not in use
MTU in VPC1460 bytes default (1500 configurable)Jumbo frames supported in some regions
Alias IP ranges per interfaceUp to 10Used for GKE pods / multi-NIC patterns

Cloud Bigtable & BigQuery Key Limits

Service Limit / Fact Exam Relevance
Bigtable — Minimum nodes1 node per clusterNot serverless; must provision nodes
Bigtable — Row key max size4 KBDesign row keys carefully; no secondary indexes
Bigtable — Column familiesUp to 100 per tableAvoid using too many families; use column qualifiers instead
BigQuery — Max query size1 MB query text; 10 GB resultsUse partitioned tables to limit scan cost
BigQuery — Streaming insert max row10 MB per row; 500 rows/requestStreaming available immediately; not for DML patterns
BigQuery — Slot reservation (Standard)100 slots baseline (on-demand)Purchase reservations for predictable workloads
Firestore — Document size1 MB per documentStore large blobs in GCS; reference from Firestore
Firestore — Writes per document1 write/second sustainedShard counters / fan-out pattern for high-write counters
GCP PCA v6.1 · High-Yield Reference

Master Trap List

60+ "if you see X, pick Y" mappings for the exam. Scan these before test day — the triggers are lifted almost verbatim from real question stems.

Compute & Containers

  • Scale to zero / pay per request, HTTPCloud Run
  • Run containers without managing serversCloud Run
  • Event-driven short function, <9 minCloud Functions Gen 1
  • Function needs >9 min or high concurrencyCloud Functions Gen 2
  • Legacy app, fixed runtime, no Docker, auto-scale to 0App Engine Standard
  • Custom runtime, Docker, background threads, websocketsApp Engine Flexible
  • Kubernetes, managed control plane, multi-zoneGKE Standard
  • Kubernetes, Google manages nodes + control planeGKE Autopilot
  • Run containers on-premises with GCP managementGoogle Distributed Cloud
  • Scheduled batch jobs, HPC, GPU, no HTTPCloud Batch
  • Managed Hadoop / Spark, ephemeral clustersDataproc
  • Need spot / preemptible VMs for batchSpot VMs (Compute Engine)
  • Sole-tenant nodes / licensing complianceSole-Tenant Nodes
  • VM manager: patch, compliance, inventoryOS Config / VM Manager
  • Need consistent performance, no noisy neighborsCompute Engine (dedicated / C2)

Storage & Databases

  • Global, strongly consistent, relational, ACIDCloud Spanner
  • Regional relational DB, managed MySQL/Postgres/SQL ServerCloud SQL
  • NoSQL, document, mobile SDK, offline syncFirestore
  • NoSQL key-value, millisecond latency, petabyte scaleCloud Bigtable
  • Time-series IoT or click-stream, high throughput writesCloud Bigtable
  • Data warehouse, OLAP, SQL analytics at scaleBigQuery
  • In-memory cache, session state, sub-millisecondMemorystore (Redis or Valkey)
  • Object store, unstructured, images/video/backupsCloud Storage
  • Shared filesystem (NFS), lift-and-shiftFilestore
  • Block storage for VMs, persistent diskPersistent Disk (PD)
  • High-performance local SSD, ephemeralLocal SSD
  • Analytics on data lake without moving dataBigLake
  • Rarely accessed cold data, 365-day minimumArchive Storage
  • Data accessed once a month, 30-day minimumNearline Storage
  • Migrate Oracle / Postgres to managed, minimal downtimeDatabase Migration Service

Networking

  • Private on-premises connectivity, <10 Gbps, managedCloud VPN (HA VPN)
  • Dedicated private connection, 10/100 Gbps, low latencyDedicated Interconnect
  • Private connection via partner, <10 Gbps, no colocationPartner Interconnect
  • 99.99% HA for VPN (correct answer)HA VPN (2 gateways, 4 tunnels)
  • Global HTTP/S load balancing, CDN, Anycast IPGlobal External Application LB
  • Internal microservices, GKE services, L7 internalInternal Application LB
  • TCP/UDP load balance, regional, preserve client IPRegional External LB (Network LB)
  • Route traffic based on content / path, internalInternal Application LB
  • Share VPC network across projects in same orgShared VPC
  • Connect two VPCs, same or different orgsVPC Network Peering
  • Dynamic routing, BGP, on-premises to GCPCloud Router
  • DDoS protection, WAF, Cloud Armor policiesCloud Armor
  • Private Google API access from on-premisesPrivate Google Access / PSC
  • DNS for internal GCP resourcesCloud DNS (private zone)
  • Content delivery, cache static assets at edgeCloud CDN
  • Inspect / filter east-west VPC trafficCloud NGFW / Firewall Policies

Security & IAM

  • Prevent data exfiltration from BigQuery / GCSVPC Service Controls
  • Customer-managed encryption keysCMEK (Cloud KMS)
  • Hardware-backed key management, FIPS 140-2 L3Cloud HSM (Cloud KMS)
  • Detect secrets / PII in dataCloud DLP (Sensitive Data Protection)
  • Scan images for vulnerabilitiesArtifact Analysis (Container Analysis)
  • VM identity, workload identity for GKE podsWorkload Identity Federation
  • Enforce org-wide security policies (e.g., no public IPs)Organization Policies
  • Verify binary authorization at deploy timeBinary Authorization
  • Audit who accessed what, compliance loggingCloud Audit Logs
  • Threat detection, anomaly detection across GCPSecurity Command Center (SCC)
  • Manage secrets / API keys centrallySecret Manager
  • Identity-aware proxy (context-aware access)Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP)
  • Service account impersonation / short-lived credentialsService Account Token Creator role
  • External identity (SAML/OIDC) to GCP resourcesWorkload Identity Federation
  • Just-in-time privileged accessPAM (Privileged Access Manager)

Operations & Cost

  • Centralized logging across projectsCloud Logging (Log Buckets + Sinks)
  • Export logs long-term, cheap archivalLog Sink → GCS (Coldline/Archive)
  • Real-time log analytics / alertingLog-based Metrics + Cloud Monitoring
  • Trace distributed microservices latencyCloud Trace
  • Application performance management, profilingCloud Profiler
  • Error tracking across servicesError Reporting
  • Infrastructure as code, declarative, idempotentTerraform / Config Connector
  • CI/CD pipelines on GCPCloud Build + Cloud Deploy
  • Artifact registry for Docker, Maven, npmArtifact Registry
  • Cost optimization: variable compute, no commitmentPreemptible / Spot VMs
  • Cost optimization: predictable usage, save 30-55%Committed Use Discounts (CUDs)
  • Cost optimization: automatic, no commitment (VMs only)Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs)
  • Move unneeded GCS objects to cheaper tier automaticallyGCS Lifecycle Rules / Autoclass
  • Centrally manage GKE clusters across environmentsFleet (GKE Hub / Connect)
  • Deploy config to many GKE clusters at onceConfig Sync / ACM

Data & Analytics Pipelines

  • Managed message queue, pub/sub, async decouplingPub/Sub
  • Exactly-once streaming ETL, Apache BeamDataflow
  • Orchestrate data pipelines, workflow schedulingCloud Composer (Airflow)
  • Real-time analytics on streaming data, SQLBigQuery (streaming inserts) / Dataflow
  • Change Data Capture from databasesDatastream
  • Managed Kafka on GCPPub/Sub (or Managed Kafka add-on)
  • Data lineage, catalog, governanceDataplex / Data Catalog
  • Trigger workflows from GCP eventsEventarc
  • Visual ETL / ELT pipeline builderCloud Data Fusion
  • Transfer large datasets from AWS / Azure / on-premStorage Transfer Service

GenAI & ML

  • Managed ML platform, training + servingVertex AI
  • Gemini API / foundation model accessVertex AI (Model Garden / Gemini)
  • Build RAG applications with search + LLMVertex AI Search (formerly Enterprise Search)
  • Conversational AI / chatbot / IVRDialogflow CX / Vertex AI Conversation
  • Image / video / speech AI APIs, no model trainingVision AI / Video AI / Speech-to-Text
  • AutoML — train with small labeled datasetVertex AI AutoML
  • Feature store for ML features, low-latency servingVertex AI Feature Store
  • ML pipeline orchestration, reproducibilityVertex AI Pipelines (Kubeflow)
  • Multi-agent AI system, tool use, reasoningVertex AI Agent Builder
  • GPU workloads, training at scaleVertex AI Training / GKE with GPUs

Migration

  • Lift-and-shift VMs from on-prem to GCPMigrate to Virtual Machines (formerly Migrate for Compute Engine)
  • Rehost containers from VMsMigrate to Containers
  • Assess migration readiness, discoveryMigration Center
  • Transfer petabytes offline, physical applianceTransfer Appliance
  • Online transfer from AWS S3 / Azure Blob to GCSStorage Transfer Service
  • Move BigQuery datasets across regionsBigQuery Data Transfer Service
Trap — Top Exam Confusions:
  • Pub/Sub vs Eventarc: Eventarc triggers Cloud Run/Workflows from GCP events natively; Pub/Sub is a general-purpose message bus you wire up yourself.
  • VPC SC vs Org Policy: VPC Service Controls restrict API access based on identity+network context; Org Policies restrict what can be created/configured (e.g., no external IPs).
  • Dataflow vs Dataproc: Dataflow (Apache Beam, fully managed, auto-scaling) for new pipelines; Dataproc (Spark/Hadoop) for existing Spark code or clusters you control.
  • Cloud SQL HA vs Spanner: Cloud SQL HA is regional (one failover replica, same region); Spanner is truly multi-region with external-consistency and 99.999% SLA.
  • Shared VPC vs Peering: Shared VPC — same org, centralized network team; Peering — different orgs or independent networks, non-transitive, no overlapping CIDRs.

More high-yield triggers (advanced)

Compute & GKE

  • pods pending but nodes exist and have headroom — autoscaler isn't adding nodesCheck HPA/VPA conflict: VPA in Auto mode restarts pods and changes requests; HPA on same CPU metric fights it — use VPA on resources + HPA on custom metrics only
  • workload needs a GPU node pool that doesn't yet exist and no node pool was pre-createdNode Auto Provisioning (NAP) — triggers on unschedulable resource requests and creates the right node pool; Cluster Autoscaler only scales existing pools
  • VPA is set but pods never get right-sized without being restarted manuallyVPA mode must be "Auto" or "Recreate" to apply recommendations; "Off" only shows recommendations, "Initial" only applies at pod creation — set mode: Auto for live right-sizing
  • batch ML training job that runs for 5 days and must not serve HTTPCloud Run Jobs — task timeout up to 168 hours (7 days), no port/HTTP endpoint needed, up to 10,000 parallel tasks; Cloud Run Services are for long-lived HTTP listeners
  • need to prevent live migration of VMs and co-locate for low latency MPIPlacement Policy (compact) — forces VMs onto same physical cluster for lowest latency; combine with sole-tenant nodes only if you also need physical host isolation from other customers
  • regulatory requirement: no Google personnel can live-migrate or access the physical hostSole-Tenant Nodes — dedicated physical server; Confidential VM encrypts memory at runtime but does NOT guarantee sole tenancy of the physical host
  • memory encryption at rest on VM RAM to protect against hypervisor compromiseConfidential VM (AMD SEV or Intel TDX) — encrypts VM memory in use; Shielded VM only verifies boot integrity (UEFI, vTPM, Secure Boot) but does not encrypt RAM
  • GKE nodes should reject all workloads except those with a specific tolerationNode Taints (NoSchedule or NoExecute) + matching Pod Tolerations — node taints are the mechanism; node labels alone do not prevent scheduling, only guide affinity rules

Storage & Databases

  • Cloud Storage bucket cost optimisation — objects vary in access frequency unpredictably over timeAutoclass — automatically transitions objects between Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive based on access patterns; lifecycle rules require you to specify fixed age/condition thresholds upfront
  • legal hold on Cloud Storage objects — must prevent any deletion or overwrite including by bucket ownerBucket Lock with Retention Policy (WORM) — once locked, even project owner cannot shorten retention or delete objects; Object Hold (legal/event-based) is per-object and can be released; Retention Policy lock is irreversible
  • PostgreSQL-compatible DB, single region, OLAP queries alongside OLTP — up to 100x faster analyticsAlloyDB with Columnar Engine — adds in-memory columnar store on top of OLTP; Cloud SQL PostgreSQL lacks a columnar engine; AlloyDB is not globally distributed (use Spanner for that)
  • Spanner schema: child table rows always fetched with parent — minimise cross-split readsTable Interleaving — physically co-locates child rows with their parent row in the same split; foreign keys enforce referential integrity but do NOT co-locate data
  • Bigtable: isolate batch analytics reads from production OLTP traffic on the same instanceApp Profiles with single-cluster routing — route batch jobs to a dedicated cluster, production to another; replication copies data; app profiles choose which cluster each app endpoint hits
  • Bigtable: very large dataset (>10 TB) with infrequent, non-latency-sensitive batch scansHDD storage tier — ~10x cheaper storage cost than SSD; storage type is permanent and per-instance; SSD is required for latency-sensitive or random-access workloads
  • Spanner also needs OLAP analytics on live operational data — globally consistentSpanner Columnar Engine (built-in) — accelerates analytical queries up to 200x on live data without ETL; AlloyDB columnar engine serves the same need but is single-region/PostgreSQL-only
  • continuous CDC replication from Cloud SQL into BigQuery for real-time analyticsDatastream — serverless CDC (change data capture) that continuously replicates; Database Migration Service (DMS) handles one-time homogeneous migrations into Cloud SQL/AlloyDB, not ongoing streaming to BQ
  • need to read same static ML model weights from many GCE VMs concurrently with maximum throughputHyperdisk ML — multi-reader mode (attach one volume to many VMs read-only), up to 2 TiB/s aggregate throughput; Hyperdisk Extreme maximises IOPS for a single writer; Filestore is NFS (higher latency)
  • block storage for latency-sensitive OLTP database — need sub-millisecond latency, single writerHyperdisk Extreme — highest IOPS (up to 350K) from one volume; Hyperdisk Balanced for mixed workloads at lower cost; Hyperdisk Throughput is for sequential/analytics (cheap but NOT sub-ms)
  • BigQuery: unpredictable bursty workloads need guaranteed minimum slots plus burst capacityReservations with Autoscaling (Editions model) — set a baseline slot count that scales automatically; Flex Slots add short-term capacity billed by the second with 60-second minimum; on-demand has no slot guarantees
  • Filestore for GKE stateful workloads requiring 99.99% availability and multi-zone resilienceFilestore Enterprise (Regional) — synchronously replicates across 3 zones, 99.99% SLA, supports Multishares; Zonal tier is single-zone 99.9% SLA; Basic tiers have no published SLA
  • ETL pipeline with visual drag-and-drop interface, no Beam/Spark coding, 200+ connectorsCloud Data Fusion — code-free IDE for ETL/ELT pipelines; Dataflow requires Apache Beam code; Datastream is CDC-only; choose Data Fusion when the team lacks streaming/Beam expertise

Networking

  • connect 20 VPC networks with transitive routing — avoid full mesh peering complexityNetwork Connectivity Center (NCC) — hub-and-spoke, transitive by design, supports up to 250 VPCs; VPC Peering is non-transitive (A↔B, B↔C does not give A↔C); Shared VPC requires host project ownership
  • service in VPC needs private IP access to a managed Google service without VPC PeeringPrivate Service Connect (PSC) — creates a single endpoint IP in your VPC for the target service; Private Service Access (PSA) uses VPC Peering under the hood and requires reserving a /16+ range; PSC avoids IP overlap problems
  • need HA VPN with 99.99% SLA — minimum topology requirementTwo HA VPN gateways each with 2 interfaces, 4 BGP tunnels total (2 per peer gateway) — configuring tunnels on only one interface of an HA VPN gateway gives 99.9%, not 99.99%
  • Dedicated Interconnect at 99.99% SLA — minimum physical circuit and metro requirements4 VLAN attachments across 2 circuits in 2 different metro locations, each metro using different edge availability domains — single-metro Interconnect achieves only 99.9%
  • encrypt Interconnect traffic at Layer 2 between on-prem router and Google edgeMACsec on Dedicated Interconnect — encrypts L2 frames between CPE and Google edge; HA VPN over Interconnect encrypts at L3/IPsec but caps each tunnel at ~3 Gbps; use MACsec when you need line-rate L2 encryption
  • enforce firewall rules hierarchically across org with override priority over project-level rulesHierarchical Firewall Policies — applied at org or folder, evaluated before VPC firewall rules; cannot be overridden by lower-level rules when action is "goto_next" vs "allow/deny"; VPC firewall rules are project-scoped only
  • internal TCP/UDP load balancing that preserves client source IP end-to-end (direct server return)Passthrough Network Load Balancer (internal) — no proxy, packets delivered unchanged, DSR for responses; proxy-based LBs (Application LB, Proxy NLB) terminate the TCP connection and open a new one to backend
  • DNS routing — send 90% of traffic to primary region, 10% to canary regionCloud DNS Weighted Round Robin (WRR) routing policy — assign weights per record set; Geolocation policy routes by client geography, not percentages; WRR and Geolocation cannot be combined on same record

Security

  • encryption key stored in your own on-premises or third-party HSM, not in Google KMSExternal Key Manager (EKM) — key material never enters Google; every crypto operation calls out to your external HSM; CMEK stores keys inside Cloud KMS (Google's infrastructure); CSEK supplies raw key bytes per-request only for GCS and Compute Engine disks
  • FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated hardware key storage inside Google Cloud KMSCloud HSM protection level within Cloud KMS — same KMS API but keys generated and stored in Google's HSMs; default Cloud KMS uses software protection level; EKM uses external HSMs outside Google
  • visibility into when Google SREs access your data vs actively approving it — key distinctionAccess Transparency (passive audit log) vs Access Approval (active customer-approval gate before Google staff can proceed) — Access Transparency requires no action from you; Access Approval blocks the Google engineer until you explicitly approve
  • grant your own engineer time-limited, just-in-time elevated IAM role with approval workflowPrivileged Access Manager (PAM) — JIT temporary role elevation with optional approver; Access Approval controls Google staff access, not your own users; IAM Conditions can restrict by time but have no approval workflow
  • block a specific IAM permission org-wide regardless of what roles are grantedIAM Deny Policy — overrides allow policies for specified permissions; Org Policy restricts resource configurations and API usage (not IAM permissions directly); VPC-SC restricts service API calls based on network context
  • prevent data exfiltration from BigQuery even if credentials are stolen — context-based perimeterVPC Service Controls — creates a service perimeter; restricts BQ API calls based on network/identity context; IAM Deny only blocks identity permissions; Org Policy restricts configurations but not network-context-based access
  • off-cluster workload (GitHub Actions CI) needs GCP access without storing a service account keyWorkload Identity Federation (external) — federates OIDC/AWS/Azure tokens to short-lived GCP credentials; GKE Workload Identity (Workload Identity Federation for GKE) is for pods inside a GKE cluster and is configured automatically by GKE — do not use it for CI pipelines
  • only allow container images with a signed attestation from your CI/CD pipeline to deploy to GKEBinary Authorization — enforces deploy-time attestation policy on GKE and Cloud Run; Key Access Justifications controls cryptographic key access for Assured Workloads, not image provenance

Operations & Cost

  • SLO alert fires too often on transient spikes — want to alert only on sustained budget consumptionMulti-window multi-burn-rate alerting — requires BOTH short window (e.g. 5 min) AND long window (e.g. 1 hr) to exceed threshold simultaneously; single-window burn-rate alerts produce high false-positive rates on short spikes
