  • error budget exhausted before end of period — what governs the response processError Budget Policy — defines what actions to take (freeze releases, increase toil budget) when error budget is depleted; SLO defines the target; SLA is the contractual commitment to customers with financial penalties
  • replacing Deployment Manager — Google's recommended successor as of 2026Infrastructure Manager — managed Terraform-based IaC service; Deployment Manager EOL is March 31, 2026; Infrastructure Manager handles state storage/locking automatically, no need for a separate GCS state bucket
  • need compute cost savings that apply across multiple regions and VM families without locking to one machine typeFlexible CUDs (spend-based) — 28% off (1yr) or 46% off (3yr) on Compute Engine/GKE/Cloud Run spend across billing account regardless of region or machine type; Resource CUDs lock to a specific region and machine family but offer up to 57–70% discount
  • guarantee VM capacity is reserved in a specific zone even if not running 24/7Reservations — guarantee zonal capacity; CUDs (flexible or resource) are discounts on usage, NOT capacity reservations; combine both if you need both guaranteed capacity and discounted pricing
  • identify which function call or code path is consuming the most CPU in a live production serviceCloud Profiler — continuous statistical CPU/heap profiling of running applications; Cloud Trace shows request latency and spans; Profiler pinpoints hot code paths, Trace shows where time is spent across service calls
  • route all audit logs from all projects to a central Security project BigQuery datasetAggregated Log Sink at organization or folder level — single sink captures logs across all child projects; project-level sinks only capture that project's logs; Log Router is the engine behind sinks

GenAI & ML

  • need RAG over enterprise docs with minimal code — managed chunking, embedding, retrievalVertex AI RAG Engine — fully managed RAG orchestration; Vector Search is a lower-level ANN index you manage yourself; Vertex AI Search is turnkey search with a UI but less customisation over chunking/embedding choices
  • semantic nearest-neighbour search over billion-scale vectors with custom embedding modelVertex AI Vector Search — low-level ANN (approximate nearest neighbour) infrastructure, you bring embeddings; RAG Engine orchestrates the full RAG pipeline on top of Vector Search or other backends
  • contact centre agent needs real-time suggested replies during a live customer callAgent Assist (CCAI) — provides real-time suggestions to human agents during calls; Conversational Agents (Dialogflow CX) powers fully automated virtual agents; CCAI Platform is the broader suite
  • generate synthetic images from text prompts in a Vertex AI pipelineImagen — Google's text-to-image model on Vertex AI; Veo is text-to-video; Chirp is speech recognition/TTS; Gemini is multimodal general reasoning — pick the purpose-built model over Gemini for image generation tasks on the exam
  • detect drift in a deployed ML model's predictions over timeVertex AI Model Monitoring — monitors prediction drift and training-serving skew on deployed endpoints; Explainable AI shows feature attributions for individual predictions (why, not drift); Model Armor filters harmful inputs/outputs at inference
  • classify and extract structured fields from scanned invoices and receipts at scaleDocument AI — purpose-built OCR + ML extractors for documents; Gemini can read documents but Document AI has pre-trained parsers (Invoice parser, Receipt parser) optimised for structured extraction at scale
  • run ML training with features that must be consistent between training and serving (no skew)Vertex AI Feature Store — centralised feature serving with point-in-time correctness; prevents training-serving skew; BigQuery ML trains directly on BQ tables but has no managed feature registry or point-in-time lookup
  • LLM responses must be grounded in your internal documents to reduce hallucinationGrounding with Vertex AI Search or RAG Engine — attaches retrieved document chunks as context to the LLM prompt; Model Monitoring detects drift post-deployment; Explainable AI explains individual predictions — neither prevents hallucination

Migration

  • assess on-premises VM estate for cloud migration fit and TCO before migratingMigration Center — discovery, assessment, and business case tool; Migrate to Virtual Machines (M2VM) is the actual migration tool; run Migration Center first to decide what to migrate and in what order
  • lift-and-shift 200 VMware VMs from on-premises to Compute Engine with minimal downtimeMigrate to Virtual Machines (M2VM) — continuous background replication then cutover; Migrate to Containers (M2C) converts VMs to container images for GKE; M2VM is the right tool for rehost (lift-and-shift), M2C for modernisation to containers
  • after VM is on GCE, want to modernise it into a GKE container workloadMigrate to Containers (M2C) — analyses the running GCE VM, extracts the app and dependencies, generates a container image and Kubernetes YAML; M2VM is for the initial VM-to-VM migration step
  • migrate a MySQL database to Cloud SQL with minimal downtime using continuous replicationDatabase Migration Service (DMS) — managed homogeneous migration with CDC replication for near-zero downtime cutover; Datastream is for ongoing CDC to analytics destinations (e.g. BigQuery), not one-time DB migration
  • transfer 500 TB of on-premises data to Cloud Storage — network transfer would take weeksTransfer Appliance — physical device shipped to your site, loaded, shipped back to Google; Storage Transfer Service is for online transfers from S3, Azure, another GCS bucket, or HTTP sources — not for bulk offline on-prem data
  • gradually replace a monolith by routing new features to microservices while old system stays liveStrangler-Fig pattern — incrementally route traffic to new services around the old monolith; Big Bang rewrite is a different (riskier) approach; use a reverse proxy or API gateway to split traffic
  • run GKE workloads across on-premises and GCP with unified policy and config managementAnthos / GKE Enterprise with Config Management — extends GKE control plane to on-prem (Anthos clusters on VMware/bare metal) with fleet-wide policy via ACM; standalone GKE is cloud-only; Anthos is the hybrid/multi-cloud answer
  • S3 bucket to Cloud Storage ongoing sync — data generated daily by a third-party serviceStorage Transfer Service — scheduled or continuous online transfers from S3, Azure Blob, HTTP, or on-prem; Transfer Appliance is a one-time offline physical transfer — not suitable for daily recurring syncs
Reference - Service Catalog

What GCP Provides

A comprehensive catalog of every GCP service a Professional Cloud Architect must know, grouped by category. Pay close attention to key limitations — exam distractors almost always exploit gaps in service constraints.

Compute

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Compute Engine IaaS VMs (custom machine types, preemptible/Spot VMs, GPUs, TPUs); managed instance groups (MIGs) with autoscaling and rolling updates Full OS control, lift-and-shift migrations, HPC, GPU/ML training, custom kernels or licensing requirements You manage the OS, patching, and agents; preemptible/Spot VMs can be reclaimed with 30-second notice; no free sustained-use discount on N2D, Tau T2D
Managed Instance Groups (MIG) Auto-healing, autoscaling, rolling updates, and multi-zone distribution of identical VM instances Stateless web tiers, batch fleets, HA across zones without manual work All instances share one template; stateful MIGs with persistent disks have slower rolling updates
Sole-Tenant Nodes Dedicated physical hosts for GCE VMs; other tenants' workloads never share the host BYOL Windows Server/SQL Server, compliance mandates requiring physical isolation (HIPAA, FedRAMP) Higher cost; must right-size to the physical node; limited availability in some regions
Bare Metal Solution Customer-dedicated physical servers co-located in Google data centers, connected via low-latency peering to GCP services Oracle RAC, SAP HANA, workloads that cannot be virtualized but need GCP proximity Not managed by GCP (no live migration, no autoscaling); long provisioning lead times; expensive minimum commitment
Preemptible / Spot VMs Surplus GCE capacity at up to 91% discount; interrupted when capacity needed elsewhere Fault-tolerant batch, rendering, ML training where jobs can checkpoint and restart Max 24-hour lifetime (preemptible); Spot can be reclaimed within 30 seconds at any time; not suitable for long-running stateful services

Containers & Serverless

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Managed Kubernetes: control-plane managed by Google, Autopilot mode fully manages node pools; integrates with Cloud Load Balancing, Workload Identity, Binary Authorization Microservices, complex orchestration, multi-container workloads, portability across clouds Autopilot restricts DaemonSets and privileged containers; Standard mode still requires node pool management and capacity planning; GKE Enterprise (formerly Anthos) adds cost
Cloud Run Fully managed serverless containers; scale-to-zero; supports HTTP, gRPC, WebSockets, and Cloud Run Jobs for batch Stateless HTTP APIs, event-driven microservices, CI/CD sidecars; cheapest path for containerized workloads with variable traffic Request timeout max 3600 seconds (1 hour); stateless (ephemeral filesystem); CPU throttled to zero when idle (unless min-instances set); max 32 GB RAM / 8 vCPU per instance
Cloud Run Jobs Run containerized batch tasks to completion; supports parallelism with task indices ETL, data processing, report generation that don't fit the HTTP request model Task timeout max 168 hours; not suitable for long-lived services that need persistent connections
Cloud Functions (2nd gen) Event-driven FaaS; triggers from Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage, Firestore, HTTP, Eventarc; powered by Cloud Run under the hood Lightweight event handlers, webhooks, glue code between services Max execution time 60 minutes (2nd gen); max memory 32 GB; cold starts can add latency; vendor lock-in on trigger APIs
App Engine Standard PaaS for web apps; auto-scales to zero; language runtimes are sandboxed (Python, Java, Go, PHP, Node.js, Ruby) Simple web apps, rapid deployment without container knowledge, automatic scaling from zero Sandbox restricts OS access, arbitrary binaries, and some system calls; limited runtime versions; cannot run arbitrary native code; max request timeout 10 minutes; no VPC-native networking without Serverless VPC Access
App Engine Flexible PaaS on GCE VMs; custom Docker containers; supports any language; no sandbox restrictions Apps needing native libraries, custom runtimes, or SSH access during debugging Minimum one VM always running (no scale-to-zero); slower cold starts; more expensive than Standard; deprecated features in some older runtimes
Eventarc Managed event routing from 130+ GCP and custom event sources to Cloud Run, Cloud Functions, and GKE using CloudEvents spec Decoupled event-driven architectures without custom Pub/Sub plumbing Targets limited to Cloud Run, Cloud Workflows, and GKE (Autopilot); some source-to-target combinations have latency SLAs, not real-time
Workflows Serverless orchestration of GCP APIs and HTTP endpoints using a YAML/JSON DSL; built-in retries, conditionals, parallel steps Multi-step automation, API chaining, saga patterns, replacing home-grown orchestrators Not a general-purpose language; complex branching logic can be awkward; max 32 KB payload per step; no native human-approval steps

Storage

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Cloud Storage Globally consistent object store; storage classes: Standard, Nearline (30-day min), Coldline (90-day min), Archive (365-day min); lifecycle rules, versioning, signed URLs, HMAC keys Unstructured data, backups, data lake, static website hosting, media, ML datasets Not a filesystem; eventual consistency for object listing after large deletes; early deletion fees for Nearline/Coldline/Archive; no append-in-place (must rewrite or compose)
Persistent Disk (PD) Durable block storage for GCE and GKE; Standard (HDD), Balanced (SSD), SSD (PD-SSD), Extreme (PD-Extreme); multi-reader mode available Boot disks, database volumes, shared read-only datasets across VMs Max size 64 TiB per disk (PD-SSD, Balanced, Extreme); 257 TiB is the per-VM aggregate limit, not a single disk; single-writer attach (except multi-reader); IOPS/throughput scales with disk size, not just instance size
Hyperdisk Next-gen block storage with independently configurable capacity, IOPS, and throughput; Hyperdisk Balanced, Extreme, ML, Throughput High-performance databases, analytics, ML training requiring predictable extreme IOPS Not compatible with all VM machine types; more expensive than PD; provisioned IOPS cost even if unused
Filestore Managed NFS file shares; Basic (HDD/SSD), Enterprise, Zonal, Regional tiers Lift-and-shift NFS workloads, shared file access for GKE (ReadWriteMany), CMS media storage NFS only (no SMB/CIFS natively); Basic tier is zonal (no HA); Enterprise is expensive; no built-in encryption key management beyond CMEK
Storage Transfer Service Managed data transfer from AWS S3, Azure Blob, on-premises, and between GCS buckets; scheduling and filtering One-time or recurring migrations, cross-cloud data replication Not real-time replication; large initial transfers can take hours/days; on-premises transfers require agent installation

Databases

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Cloud SQL Managed MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server; automatic failover, read replicas, point-in-time recovery, IAM auth OLTP relational workloads that don't need horizontal write scaling; modernize on-premises RDBMS No horizontal write scaling (single primary only); max storage 64 TB per instance; cannot decrease storage size without recreating instance; up to 128 vCPU / 864 GB RAM (Enterprise Plus, performance-optimized)
AlloyDB for PostgreSQL PostgreSQL-compatible; columnar engine for analytics queries; up to 4x faster OLTP than Cloud SQL; auto-managed storage; AI/ML embedding functions built-in High-performance PostgreSQL OLTP with analytical mixed workloads; vector search for AI apps PostgreSQL only (no MySQL/SQL Server); minimum instance 2 vCPU / 16 GB RAM; ~39% premium over Cloud SQL Enterprise; VPC Peering connectivity (non-transitive)
Cloud Spanner Globally distributed, strongly consistent, horizontally scalable relational database; SQL and mutations API; TrueTime for external consistency Global OLTP requiring horizontal write scaling, strong consistency, and 99.999% SLA; gaming, finance, global inventory Significantly more expensive than Cloud SQL (~$65/month minimum at 100 PUs; ~$900/month per node); requires schema design for hotspot avoidance; no PostgreSQL wire protocol (uses proprietary API unless using PGAdapter); small instances (<1000 PU) have non-linear scaling
Cloud Firestore Serverless, fully managed document NoSQL database; real-time sync for mobile/web SDKs; offline support; ACID transactions per document; Native and Datastore modes Mobile/web backends, real-time collaboration, hierarchical data, user profiles, game state Supports range/inequality filters on up to 10 fields (composite index required); no native full-text search; document size capped at 1 MiB; write rate limit ~1 write/sec per document; composite indexes must be pre-defined; queries always index-backed
Cloud Bigtable Managed wide-column NoSQL at petabyte scale; HBase API compatible; consistent single-digit ms latency; ideal for time-series and IoT Time-series, IoT telemetry, AdTech click streams, financial market data — all high-volume, low-latency key-value at massive scale No SQL, no joins, no multi-row transactions, no secondary indexes; minimum one node per cluster (~$470/month at SSD pricing); schema design is critical — row key hotspots degrade performance; no ad-hoc queries
Memorystore for Redis Managed Redis 6/7; Standard tier (HA with replica); Basic tier (no HA); Memorystore for Redis Cluster for horizontal scale Session caching, leaderboards, pub/sub fan-out, rate limiting, real-time analytics Data is in-memory; max 300 GB per standalone instance (1 TB quota per region); Basic tier has no HA; persistence options (RDB/AOF) add latency; data lost on instance failure without persistence
Memorystore for Memcached Managed Memcached clusters; multi-node sharding; AUTO_DISCOVERY for client-side node discovery Simple read caching for apps already using Memcached; stateless cache that doesn't need persistence or pub/sub No persistence; no pub/sub; no data structures beyond key-value; not suitable for session storage requiring durability
Database Migration Service (DMS) Minimal-downtime migrations to Cloud SQL and AlloyDB using CDC (change data capture); supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, Oracle Lift-and-shift database migrations with minimal production downtime Requires network connectivity from source to Google; some DDL changes require migration restart; Oracle migrations require additional licensing and tooling

Data & Analytics

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
BigQuery Serverless, columnar data warehouse; petabyte-scale SQL analytics; built-in ML (BigQuery ML); BI Engine for in-memory acceleration; federated queries to GCS, Bigtable, Spanner; real-time ingestion via streaming inserts OLAP, business intelligence, ad-hoc analytics, data science, log analysis; best choice when data exceeds what fits in a relational DB and query latency of seconds is acceptable Not for low-latency OLTP (DML is expensive, not transactional in the RDBMS sense); streaming inserts cost more than batch load; queries billed per bytes scanned (use table partitioning and clustering to control costs); slot-based reservations for predictable pricing require up-front commitment
Dataflow Managed Apache Beam service for batch and streaming ETL/ELT pipelines; auto-scaling workers; exactly-once processing semantics Real-time stream processing, ETL from Pub/Sub to BigQuery/GCS, complex windowing and joins; when you need exactly-once guarantees Beam SDK learning curve; job startup latency (1–3 min) makes it poor for sub-second latency; per-vCPU-hour pricing; Flex Templates required for custom environments
Pub/Sub Fully managed, globally distributed message queue and streaming service; at-least-once delivery; up to 7-day message retention; push and pull subscriptions; Pub/Sub Lite for cost-optimized zonal delivery Event ingestion, fan-out, decoupling microservices, Dataflow source/sink, IoT data pipelines At-least-once (not exactly-once); ordering guaranteed only within a message ordering key; Pub/Sub Lite is zonal (not global) and has lower SLA; no dead-letter queue natively without explicit configuration
Dataproc Managed Hadoop, Spark, Hive, Presto clusters; on-demand cluster creation; GCS as HDFS replacement; Dataproc Serverless for Spark without cluster management Migrating existing Hadoop/Spark workloads, Spark ML, Hive queries, batch ETL on existing ecosystems Cluster management still required for non-serverless; cluster startup takes 60–90 seconds; cost scales with cluster size even when idle; not ideal for net-new pipelines where Dataflow is more managed
Looker / Looker Studio Looker: enterprise BI platform with LookML semantic model, governed metrics; Looker Studio (free): drag-and-drop dashboards connected to BigQuery, Sheets, etc. Self-service BI, consistent metric definitions across teams, embedded analytics in applications Looker (full) is enterprise-priced and complex to model; Looker Studio lacks governance and row-level security at scale; both require BigQuery or other connector costs
Dataplex Intelligent data fabric for data governance, metadata management, data quality, and data lineage across GCS, BigQuery, and Dataproc Enterprise data mesh, unified discovery of data assets, automated data quality checks, policy tagging Relatively new; some features (lineage, quality) have limited depth versus dedicated tools; adds complexity for small teams; cost scales with data scanned for discovery
Data Catalog Managed metadata service and data discovery; tag templates, search across BigQuery, GCS, Pub/Sub, Bigtable; now integrated into Dataplex Discoverability, classification, and tagging of data assets for governance and compliance Not a data store; search is metadata only; tagging policy enforcement requires additional IAM and DLP integration
Cloud Data Fusion Managed CDAP-based ETL/ELT platform with a visual pipeline builder; 150+ connectors; runs on Dataproc Business-analyst-friendly ETL, migrating on-premises SSIS/Informatica pipelines to GCP Expensive compared to writing Dataflow directly; underlying Dataproc clusters add cold-start latency; not suitable for real-time sub-second pipelines

Networking

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) Global, software-defined network; subnets are regional; auto or custom mode; firewall rules, routes, flow logs; shared VPC for cross-project networking Foundation for all GCP workloads; shared VPC for centralized network control in multi-project organizations VPC Peering is non-transitive (A-B and B-C peering does NOT give A-C connectivity; use Shared VPC or NCC instead); max 7000 routes per VPC; dynamic routing requires Cloud Router
Cloud Load Balancing Fully managed, globally distributed load balancing; External HTTP(S) LB (global Anycast), Internal HTTP(S) LB, Network LB (TCP/UDP), Internal TCP/UDP LB; Cloud CDN integration; SSL offload Scale web traffic globally, HA with health checks, SSL termination, URL-based routing Premium Network Tier required for global external LB; Standard tier routes traffic regionally; Backend services must be in same region for internal LB; connection draining default 300 sec
Cloud CDN Anycast CDN built on Google's edge PoPs; cache GCS objects and backend responses; signed URLs; cache invalidation Static asset delivery, video streaming, API response caching, reducing origin latency globally Only works with external HTTP(S) LB backends; cache invalidation can take time to propagate; large objects (>10 GB) require chunked transfer; HTTPS required for caching to work correctly
Cloud DNS Managed authoritative DNS; private zones for internal GCP resolution; public zones; DNS policies; DNSSEC; forwarding zones for hybrid environments Hosting public DNS, internal service discovery, hybrid on-premises-to-GCP DNS resolution Not a global load balancer; DNS TTLs can delay propagation; DNSSEC requires zone signing setup; forwarding zones require connectivity to on-premises resolvers
Cloud VPN (HA VPN) IPsec VPN over the internet; HA VPN provides 99.99% SLA with two redundant tunnels; BGP routing via Cloud Router Connecting on-premises networks to GCP when Interconnect is not justified by cost or timeline Max ~3 Gbps throughput per HA VPN tunnel (limited by packet rate); internet path adds latency and jitter; not suitable for consistent high-bandwidth requirements; no SLA without BGP and HA topology
Cloud Interconnect Dedicated Interconnect: 10 or 100 Gbps private circuits from colocation facility to Google; Partner Interconnect: through service providers from 50 Mbps to 50 Gbps High-bandwidth, low-latency, consistent hybrid connectivity; large data transfer volumes where egress costs make VPN impractical Traffic is NOT encrypted by default (private circuit, not IPsec); must add HA VPN over Interconnect for encryption; Dedicated requires colocation presence; Partner has dependency on provider SLA; provisioning takes weeks
Network Connectivity Center (NCC) Hub-and-spoke network topology for connecting VPCs, on-premises, and other clouds via a central hub; enables transitive routing between spokes Replace complex VPC Peering full-mesh with scalable hub-and-spoke; multi-cloud and hybrid connectivity at enterprise scale Data transfer fees between spokes; some spoke types (e.g., VLAN attachments) have limited inter-spoke routing; relatively new service with evolving feature set
Cloud Armor DDoS protection and WAF integrated with Cloud Load Balancing; pre-configured WAF rules (OWASP Top 10), rate limiting, adaptive protection, geo-based IP allow/deny Protect web apps from DDoS, SQLi, XSS, layer 7 attacks; bot management Only works with external HTTP(S) LB and TCP proxy LB; advanced tiers (Managed Protection Plus) are expensive; rules are evaluated in priority order and can be complex to manage at scale
Private Service Connect Private endpoint in your VPC for consuming managed GCP services (BigQuery, GCS, etc.) or third-party services without public internet traversal Compliance requirements to keep traffic off the internet; connecting to published services (e.g., AlloyDB, Cloud SQL) from peered VPCs Not all GCP services support PSC endpoints yet; consumer and producer must configure endpoints separately; no transitive connectivity

Security & IAM

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Cloud IAM Identity and Access Management; principals (users, service accounts, groups, workload identity), roles (basic, predefined, custom), conditions, deny policies, IAM Recommender All GCP access control; prefer predefined roles over basic; use Workload Identity for GKE pods instead of service account keys Max 1500 custom roles per project/org; IAM conditions have limited expression capabilities; IAM changes can take up to 60 seconds to propagate; service account key management is a security anti-pattern
Cloud KMS Managed key management; software-backed (Cloud KMS) and hardware-backed (Cloud HSM) key versions; CMEK for encrypting GCP service data; key rotation, versioning, import Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) for compliance; key rotation policies; audit trails of key use Keys are regional (choose region wisely for latency and compliance); Cloud HSM has higher cost and limited regional availability; key destruction is irreversible after 24-hour scheduled deletion window
Secret Manager Managed secrets store for API keys, passwords, certificates; versioned; automatic replication; IAM-gated access; audit logs; TTL and rotation integration Storing database credentials, API keys, TLS certificates securely; replaces environment variable secrets Max 10,000 secrets per project; secret versions are immutable; rotation must be triggered externally (no native automatic rotation beyond Pub/Sub notification triggers)
VPC Service Controls Security perimeters around GCP services to prevent data exfiltration; restricts which VPCs/identities can call specific APIs regardless of IAM Data exfiltration prevention for regulated data (PII, PHI, financial); layered defense beyond IAM Complex to configure and test; "dry run" mode required before enforcement to avoid breaking legitimate access; not all GCP services support VPC-SC perimeters; access context policies can be confusing
Security Command Center (SCC) Centralized security and risk management platform; vulnerability findings, misconfiguration detection, threat detection (Event Threat Detection), compliance dashboards (CIS, PCI, HIPAA) Org-wide security posture management, vulnerability scanning, SIEM integration Premium tier required for threat detection and compliance reporting (significant cost); findings are not real-time for all detectors; requires org-level activation
Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) Zero-trust access gateway for web apps and VMs; verifies user identity and context before allowing access; replaces VPN for internal tools BeyondCorp-style access to internal apps without a VPN; securing internal web UIs Only supports HTTP/HTTPS; does not replace VPN for all protocols; requires IAP-aware app or IAP connector; context-aware access requires additional Chrome Enterprise or Endpoint Verification
Binary Authorization Deploy-time policy enforcement for container images; attestors sign images after security checks; enforced on GKE, Cloud Run, and GCF Supply chain security, ensuring only verified images run in production Attestation pipeline adds CI/CD complexity; emergencies require break-glass procedures; does not scan for runtime vulnerabilities
Cloud Audit Logs Immutable audit trail of who did what, where, and when; Admin Activity (always on), Data Access (opt-in), System Event, Policy Denied logs per service Compliance, forensics, incident investigation; required for SOC 2, PCI, HIPAA Data Access logs can generate very high volume and cost; must explicitly enable for each service; default retention 400 days for Admin Activity, 30 days for Data Access; exports to Cloud Storage/BigQuery for long-term retention

AI / ML / Generative AI

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Vertex AI (Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform) End-to-end MLOps platform: Model Garden (200+ foundation models including Gemini), Workbench (managed notebooks), Pipelines (Kubeflow), Feature Store, Model Registry, Prediction endpoints, Vector Search, AutoML, Agent Builder Enterprise ML training, fine-tuning, serving; RAG applications; agent orchestration; all ML workloads requiring MLOps governance Complex platform with steep learning curve; prediction endpoints have cold start latency; online prediction costs more than batch; Gemini API quotas can throttle high-traffic apps; feature store has ingestion latency
Gemini Models (API) Gemini 2.0 / 2.5 family: Flash (fast, cheap), Pro (balanced), Ultra (most capable); multimodal (text, image, audio, video, code); long context windows (up to 1M tokens) Generative AI applications, RAG, summarization, code generation, multimodal analysis Token limits and rate limits per project; model responses are non-deterministic; data residency constraints on which regions serve requests; cost scales with token volume
Vertex AI Search Managed RAG and enterprise search over structured and unstructured data (GCS, BigQuery, websites); grounding for Gemini responses; pre-built UI components Grounded GenAI apps that need to retrieve enterprise data before generating responses Indexing latency (not real-time); limited control over embedding models vs. custom RAG; data connector support varies by source type
Vertex AI Agent Builder Low-code platform to build, deploy, and manage conversational AI agents backed by Gemini; integrates with Vertex AI Search for grounding Customer-facing chatbots, virtual agents, internal Q&A bots backed by enterprise knowledge bases Limited customization vs. building agents directly with Gemini API; latency can be higher than direct API calls; limited support for complex multi-turn stateful flows
AutoML No-code/low-code ML training for tabular, image, video, text, and translation tasks; integrated into Vertex AI Teams without ML expertise needing custom models; rapid prototyping; small-to-medium datasets Limited model customization vs. custom training; may underperform bespoke architectures on complex tasks; training time and cost can be significant for large datasets
Cloud Natural Language API Pre-trained NLP: sentiment analysis, entity recognition, entity sentiment, syntax analysis, content classification Quick NLP features without training a custom model; content moderation, social media analysis Pre-trained only; limited to supported languages; cannot fine-tune; Gemini API now covers most use cases with better accuracy
Cloud Vision API Pre-trained image analysis: label detection, OCR, face detection, object localization, safe search, logo detection Quick image analysis, document OCR, content moderation at scale without custom training Pre-trained models may not match domain-specific accuracy; limited fine-tuning; Gemini multimodal covers many overlapping use cases
Document AI Managed document processing: OCR, form parsing, invoice processing, contract analysis; pre-trained parsers and custom document processors Automating document ingestion for invoices, receipts, tax forms, contracts, medical records Accuracy varies by document quality and type; custom processors require labeled training data; per-page pricing adds up at scale

Operations & Observability

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Cloud Monitoring Metrics collection, dashboards, alerting, uptime checks; workspace-based multi-project view; 1500+ built-in metrics; custom metrics via OpenTelemetry or Monitoring API GCP-native monitoring for all services; SLO/SLI tracking; alert policies with notification channels Default metric retention 6 weeks; custom metrics have per-metric costs; alerting has a maximum of 500 alert policies per project; advanced alerting (Monitoring Query Language) has a learning curve
Cloud Logging Centralized log management; _Default and _Required log buckets; log-based metrics; log sinks to GCS, BigQuery, Pub/Sub; log analytics (SQL over logs) Centralized log aggregation, compliance log retention, creating metrics from logs, debugging distributed systems Default retention 30 days (_Default bucket); log exclusion filters needed to control cost; high-volume workloads can generate significant ingestion costs; search is not full-text by default
Cloud Trace Distributed tracing; automatic instrumentation for Cloud Run, GKE; OpenTelemetry compatible; latency analysis, trace comparison Identifying latency bottlenecks in distributed microservices Sampling rates must be tuned to balance cost and coverage; trace data retention is 30 days; limited built-in anomaly detection vs. third-party APM tools
Cloud Profiler Continuous CPU and heap profiling of production workloads with minimal overhead; flame graphs; supports Java, Go, Python, Node.js Performance optimization of long-running services; finding memory leaks and CPU hotspots in production Agent must be instrumented in application code; not all runtimes supported equally; profiling data is statistical (sampling), not deterministic
Error Reporting Aggregates and deduplicates application errors from Cloud Logging; groups similar errors, shows frequency, alerts on new error classes Rapid detection of new production errors; prioritization by error frequency and impact Requires structured error format in logs; limited root cause analysis vs. full APM; free tier is basic; does not trace errors across service boundaries

DevOps & IaC

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Cloud Build Managed CI/CD build service; triggers from Cloud Source Repositories, GitHub, Bitbucket; Docker and custom build steps; integration with Artifact Registry and GKE GCP-native CI pipelines, container builds, automated test execution, deploy pipelines Max 24-hour build timeout; concurrent build limits per project; no native build queuing priority; limited compared to full CI platforms (Jenkins, GitHub Actions) for complex workflows
Cloud Deploy Managed, opinionated continuous delivery for GKE, Cloud Run, and GCE; delivery pipelines with stages, approvals, rollback, canary/blue-green strategies Progressive delivery with automated rollouts and rollback; GCP-native CD with audit trails for compliance Targets limited to GKE, Cloud Run, and GCE (no other runtimes); opinionated pipeline structure may not fit all deployment patterns; requires Skaffold configuration
Artifact Registry Managed artifact repository for Docker images, Maven, npm, Python, Go, APT, YUM; regional; integrated with Cloud Build and GKE; vulnerability scanning Storing build artifacts, container images, private npm/Maven packages within GCP Regional (no built-in global replication without manual config); storage costs scale with artifact size and retention; replaces Container Registry (deprecated)
Cloud Source Repositories Managed Git repositories hosted on GCP; integration with Cloud Build triggers; free for small teams Keeping source code within GCP for compliance; simple repos without need for GitHub/GitLab features Lacks GitHub/GitLab features (pull request review UI, Actions ecosystem, integrations); mostly used as a mirror; most teams prefer GitHub/GitLab with Cloud Build triggers
Terraform (on GCP) Open-source IaC; Google provider manages all GCP resources; Cloud Foundation Toolkit provides opinionated Terraform modules; now an explicit PCA exam requirement Infrastructure-as-code for reproducible, version-controlled GCP environments; required for enterprise governance State management requires GCS backend; Terraform Cloud or self-managed state locking needed for teams; provider updates can introduce breaking changes; not a GCP-managed service
Config Connector Kubernetes operator that manages GCP resources (Cloud SQL, Pub/Sub, IAM) as Kubernetes CRDs; enables GitOps for GCP infrastructure via GKE Teams running GKE who want to manage GCP resources using kubectl and GitOps workflows Requires GKE; CRD lifecycle management adds complexity; not all GCP resources are supported; slower than Terraform for large resource graphs

Migration & Hybrid

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Migrate to Virtual Machines Lift-and-shift VMs from VMware, AWS, or Azure to GCE; test clones without disrupting production; streaming migration (low downtime) Fast migration of existing VM workloads to GCP without re-platforming Migrated VMs are still VMs (no modernization); Windows licensing costs carry over; requires network connectivity from source; not suitable for containerizing workloads
GKE Enterprise (Anthos) Multi-cloud and hybrid Kubernetes management; GKE clusters on-premises (bare metal/VMware), on AWS/Azure; Config Management, Service Mesh (Istio), Policy Controller, Fleet management Running Kubernetes consistently across on-premises and multi-cloud; centralized policy and service mesh management Complex and expensive; requires GKE Enterprise license; on-premises clusters need significant infrastructure; managing fleet of clusters adds operational overhead
Transfer Appliance Physical hardware appliance shipped to customer site to ingest up to 1 PB of data per appliance for offline transfer to GCS Large-scale data migrations where network bandwidth is insufficient or too costly; >20 TB datasets Long round-trip time (shipping + ingestion); limited to GCS as destination; requires careful data preparation and encryption; not for ongoing replication
Cloud Interconnect (hybrid) See Networking section — dedicated private connectivity between on-premises and GCP High-bandwidth, consistent hybrid workloads; DR replication; SAP/Oracle data transfer Not encrypted by default; requires colocation or partner provider; weeks to provision

Management & Governance

Service What it provides Why / when to choose it Key limitation
Resource Manager Hierarchical organization of GCP resources: Organization > Folders > Projects; IAM and policy inheritance; labels; project lifecycle management Structuring multi-team, multi-environment GCP estates; applying org-wide policies; billing separation Folder depth limit (10 levels); moving projects between folders may briefly disrupt billing; org policy constraints must be tested before enforcement
Organization Policy Service Centralized policy constraints on GCP resource configuration (e.g., restrict regions, disable public IPs, require Shielded VMs, enforce CMEK) Preventive guardrails for compliance and security; stopping misconfigurations before they happen Policies inherited down the hierarchy cannot be selectively overridden at lower levels without exceptions; some constraints only apply to new resources, not existing; complex constraint conditions require Common Expression Language
Cloud Billing Billing account management, budget alerts, cost exports to BigQuery, committed use discounts (CUDs), sustained use discounts (SUDs); billing subaccounts for resellers Cost management, chargebacks, budget enforcement, identifying cost anomalies Budgets are alerts only (no automatic resource shutdown unless combined with Cloud Functions); CUD commitments are 1 or 3 years; detailed billing data available only via BigQuery export (not real-time)
Cloud Asset Inventory Real-time inventory of all GCP resources and IAM policies; export to BigQuery or GCS; change history; policy analysis Compliance audits, understanding resource sprawl, detecting IAM policy changes across an org Export and analysis may have slight delay; complex queries require BigQuery knowledge; not a real-time alerting system (use SCC or Audit Logs for that)
Apigee API Management Enterprise API gateway and management platform; API proxies, rate limiting, monetization, developer portal, analytics; supports REST, SOAP, gRPC Publishing and managing APIs at scale; API monetization; partner ecosystem; enterprise API governance Expensive (enterprise pricing); complex to configure for simple use cases; Cloud Endpoints is simpler for GCP-internal API management; requires separate operational expertise
Cloud Endpoints Lightweight API management using Envoy proxy; OpenAPI and gRPC support; authentication, monitoring, logging; deployed on Cloud Run, GKE, or GCE Adding auth, monitoring, and quotas to APIs without the cost/complexity of Apigee Less featured than Apigee (no monetization, limited developer portal, basic analytics); configuration is code-based and requires redeploy for changes
How to use limitations to eliminate wrong exam answers: The PCA exam frequently presents scenarios where multiple services could theoretically work but one has a disqualifying limitation. Apply this elimination process: (1) Scale — if the question involves horizontal write scaling, Cloud SQL is wrong; choose Spanner. (2) Latency — if the question requires sub-millisecond or consistent low-latency key lookup at massive scale, BigQuery is wrong; choose Bigtable or Memorystore. (3) Cost floor — if the scenario is a startup with minimal budget, Bigtable (min ~$470/month) and Spanner (min ~$65–900/month) may be over-engineered; Cloud SQL or Firestore may be correct. (4) Connectivity — if a question asks about connecting three or more VPCs transitively, VPC Peering is wrong; choose Shared VPC or NCC. (5) Encryption in transit — if the question requires encrypted hybrid connectivity, Cloud Interconnect alone is wrong (add HA VPN over Interconnect). (6) Serverless runtime — if the app needs OS-level access or persistent filesystem, Cloud Run and App Engine Standard are wrong; choose GKE or GCE. (7) OLTP vs OLAP — BigQuery is never correct for low-latency transactional workloads; choose Cloud SQL, AlloyDB, or Spanner. (8) Query patterns — Firestore has no native full-text search (use external search); inequality filters span up to 10 fields with composite indexes; Bigtable cannot do joins or secondary indexes.
Reference - Cloud Equivalents

GCP vs AWS vs Azure

Service-to-service mapping across the three major clouds. Use this to quickly orient if you come from an AWS or Azure background, and to understand where GCP has genuine differentiators versus parity services.

Compute

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Virtual Machines Compute Engine EC2 Azure Virtual Machines GCP offers custom machine types (any vCPU/RAM combination); AWS has largest instance variety; Azure tight integration with Windows Server and Active Directory
VM Autoscaling Managed Instance Groups (MIG) Auto Scaling Groups (ASG) Virtual Machine Scale Sets (VMSS) GCP MIGs support rolling updates and auto-healing natively; AWS ASG integrates tightly with ALB target groups; Azure VMSS supports flexible and uniform orchestration modes
Spot / Preemptible VMs Spot VMs / Preemptible VMs Spot Instances Azure Spot VMs GCP Spot VMs have no 2-minute notice (just 30 seconds); AWS Spot has 2-minute interruption notice and Spot Instance Advisor; Azure Spot supports eviction policies (deallocate or delete)
Dedicated hosts Sole-Tenant Nodes EC2 Dedicated Hosts Azure Dedicated Hosts All three offer physical isolation for licensing and compliance; AWS supports BYOL most broadly; GCP Sole-Tenant integrates with Committed Use Discounts
High-performance / bare metal Bare Metal Solution EC2 Bare Metal instances Azure BareMetal Infrastructure GCP Bare Metal is co-located in Google facilities connected via peering; AWS bare metal runs in standard AZs; Azure BareMetal targets SAP HANA specifically

Containers & Kubernetes

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Managed Kubernetes GKE (Standard & Autopilot) EKS AKS GKE invented Kubernetes and has the most mature managed offering; GKE Autopilot fully manages nodes (pay per pod, not node); EKS is highly configurable with Fargate option; AKS is deeply integrated with Azure AD and free control plane
Serverless containers Cloud Run AWS Fargate (on ECS/EKS), App Runner Azure Container Apps Cloud Run scales to zero natively and is the simplest container serverless experience; Fargate requires ECS/EKS cluster setup; App Runner is simpler than Fargate but less flexible; Azure Container Apps supports KEDA-based event-driven scaling
Container registry Artifact Registry ECR (Elastic Container Registry) Azure Container Registry (ACR) GCP Artifact Registry also hosts Maven, npm, Python packages (not just Docker); ECR integrates tightly with ECS/EKS; ACR supports geo-replication in Premium tier
Multi-cloud / hybrid Kubernetes GKE Enterprise (Anthos) EKS Anywhere, EKS on Outposts Azure Arc GKE Enterprise (Anthos) is the most comprehensive multi-cloud K8s platform; Azure Arc supports any Kubernetes cluster (not just AKS); EKS Anywhere runs on-premises; Azure Arc also extends to servers and data services

Serverless Functions & App Platforms

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Functions as a Service (FaaS) Cloud Functions (2nd gen) AWS Lambda Azure Functions GCF 2nd gen runs on Cloud Run (up to 60 min timeout, 32 GB RAM); Lambda has largest ecosystem of triggers and 15-min max; Azure Functions supports Durable Functions for stateful orchestration
PaaS (managed runtime) App Engine AWS Elastic Beanstalk Azure App Service App Engine Standard has strictest sandbox (no arbitrary binaries); Elastic Beanstalk is more configurable; Azure App Service integrates with Azure DevOps and has built-in deployment slots for blue/green
Workflow orchestration Workflows AWS Step Functions Azure Logic Apps / Durable Functions Google Workflows uses a YAML/JSON DSL; Step Functions uses ASL (JSON) and has more connector integrations; Logic Apps is low-code with 400+ connectors; Durable Functions is code-first in Azure Functions

Object, Block & File Storage

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Object storage Cloud Storage (GCS) S3 Azure Blob Storage GCS has strong global consistency; S3 has the most mature ecosystem and most third-party integrations; Azure Blob has lifecycle tiers (Hot/Cool/Cold/Archive); all three have similar pricing but egress costs differ significantly
Block storage Persistent Disk / Hyperdisk EBS (gp3, io2) Azure Managed Disks (Standard HDD/SSD, Premium SSD, Ultra Disk) GCP Hyperdisk allows independently configuring IOPS and throughput from capacity; AWS EBS gp3 decouples IOPS from size; Azure Ultra Disk offers up to 160,000 IOPS per disk
Managed NFS / file storage Filestore EFS (NFS), FSx (Windows/Lustre/NetApp) Azure Files (SMB/NFS) Filestore is NFS only; AWS EFS is NFS with automatic scaling; FSx family offers Windows SMB and Lustre; Azure Files supports both SMB and NFS with Azure Active Directory auth
Offline bulk data transfer Transfer Appliance AWS Snowball / Snowmobile Azure Data Box AWS Snowball Edge has edge compute capability; Snowmobile handles exabyte scale (truck); GCP Transfer Appliance tops at ~1 PB; Azure Data Box available in several sizes up to 1 PB

Relational Databases

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Managed relational DB Cloud SQL (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server) RDS (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, Oracle, MariaDB) Azure SQL Database / Azure Database for MySQL/PostgreSQL GCP Cloud SQL max 64 TB; AWS RDS supports Oracle (GCP does not have managed Oracle); Azure SQL Database has serverless tier that pauses when idle; all support read replicas but no horizontal write scaling
High-performance PostgreSQL AlloyDB for PostgreSQL Aurora PostgreSQL Azure Flexible Server (PostgreSQL) / Hyperscale Citus AlloyDB is GCP's Aurora equivalent; both use disaggregated storage with read replicas; Aurora has broader ecosystem and longer track record; AlloyDB adds built-in ML/vector search functions; Citus (Azure) adds sharding for PostgreSQL
Globally distributed relational DB Cloud Spanner Aurora Global Database Azure Cosmos DB for PostgreSQL (Citus) / Azure SQL Hyperscale Spanner is the only service offering external consistency (TrueTime) with horizontal write scaling globally; Aurora Global has a primary region with read replicas globally (writes still single region); Cosmos DB for PostgreSQL uses Citus sharding but is not a traditional relational DB

NoSQL Databases

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Serverless document DB Cloud Firestore DynamoDB Azure Cosmos DB (Core/SQL API) Firestore has strong real-time sync SDKs for mobile/web; DynamoDB is the most mature serverless NoSQL with single-digit ms SLA; Cosmos DB offers multiple APIs (SQL, MongoDB, Cassandra, Gremlin, Table) with multi-region writes
Wide-column / time-series NoSQL Cloud Bigtable DynamoDB (also wide-column use cases), Keyspaces (managed Cassandra) Azure Cosmos DB for Cassandra Bigtable is HBase-compatible and purpose-built for petabyte-scale time-series; AWS Keyspaces (Cassandra) is serverless; Azure Cosmos DB for Cassandra is serverless Cassandra API; Bigtable has lowest latency at extreme scale but highest minimum cost

Data Warehouse & Analytics

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Cloud data warehouse BigQuery Redshift Azure Synapse Analytics BigQuery is fully serverless (no cluster management, pay per query or flat-rate slots); Redshift requires cluster provisioning (Redshift Serverless is an option but less mature); Synapse combines data warehouse + Spark + data integration; BigQuery has fastest cold-query startup of the three
Managed Spark / Hadoop Dataproc / Dataproc Serverless EMR (Elastic MapReduce) Azure HDInsight / Azure Databricks GCP Dataproc uses GCS instead of HDFS; Dataproc Serverless removes cluster management for Spark; AWS EMR integrates with Graviton instances for cost; Azure Databricks (joint venture) is the most full-featured Spark platform across clouds
BI / visualization Looker / Looker Studio QuickSight Power BI Looker has the most sophisticated semantic modeling (LookML); QuickSight is cost-effective for AWS-native orgs; Power BI is dominant in Microsoft shops with deep Excel/Teams integration

Cache

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Managed Redis Memorystore for Redis ElastiCache for Redis Azure Cache for Redis All three offer managed Redis with HA; GCP max 300 GB standalone (Redis Cluster for horizontal scale); ElastiCache supports Redis 7.x with cluster mode; Azure Cache has Enterprise tier with Redis on Flash (hybrid DRAM+SSD) for larger datasets
Managed Memcached Memorystore for Memcached ElastiCache for Memcached No direct managed equivalent AWS ElastiCache for Memcached supports Auto Discovery; GCP Memorystore for Memcached also has Auto Discovery; Azure lacks a managed Memcached service (use Redis instead)

Messaging & Streaming

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Managed message / event streaming Pub/Sub Kinesis Data Streams, SNS, SQS Azure Event Hubs, Azure Service Bus Pub/Sub is a single service combining queue (SQS-like pull) and push fan-out (SNS-like); Kinesis is partition-based with shard management; SQS is simpler queue; Event Hubs uses Kafka protocol; Service Bus is enterprise messaging with topics/queues; Pub/Sub has no shard/partition management
Managed Kafka Pub/Sub (Kafka API via Confluent on GCP) MSK (Managed Streaming for Kafka) Azure Event Hubs (Kafka protocol) GCP does not have a native managed Kafka (Confluent Cloud on GCP fills this gap); AWS MSK is fully managed Kafka; Azure Event Hubs exposes a Kafka-compatible API without running actual Kafka brokers

ETL & Data Integration

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Managed stream & batch ETL Dataflow (Apache Beam) AWS Glue (Spark-based), Kinesis Data Firehose (streaming) Azure Data Factory (ADF), Azure Stream Analytics Dataflow uses Apache Beam and offers exactly-once semantics; Glue is PySpark-based with a data catalog; Firehose is simpler for stream-to-store delivery; ADF is a visual ETL tool closer to Cloud Data Fusion than Dataflow; Stream Analytics is SQL-based streaming
Visual ETL / low-code pipeline Cloud Data Fusion AWS Glue Studio Azure Data Factory All three offer visual pipeline designers; ADF is the most mature with 90+ connectors; Cloud Data Fusion runs on Dataproc (adds cluster latency); Glue Studio generates PySpark code

Networking & VPC

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Virtual private network VPC (global, subnets are regional) VPC (regional, AZ-scoped subnets) Azure Virtual Network (VNet, regional) GCP VPC is global — a single VPC spans all regions; AWS VPCs are regional with AZ subnets; Azure VNets are regional; GCP's global VPC simplifies multi-region designs but requires careful firewall rule management
Multi-VPC / transitive routing Network Connectivity Center / Shared VPC Transit Gateway Azure Virtual WAN / VNet Peering with Gateway Transit GCP VPC Peering is non-transitive; NCC provides hub-and-spoke transitive routing; AWS Transit Gateway is the gold standard for hub-and-spoke; Azure Virtual WAN provides managed SD-WAN with transit routing
Load balancing Cloud Load Balancing (global HTTP(S), Network, Internal) ALB (Application), NLB (Network), CLB (Classic), GWLB Azure Application Gateway, Azure Load Balancer, Azure Front Door GCP external HTTP(S) LB is global Anycast (single IP routes worldwide); AWS ALB is regional; Azure Front Door provides global HTTP routing similar to GCP's global LB; Azure Application Gateway is regional WAF+LB
DDoS / WAF Cloud Armor AWS WAF + AWS Shield Azure WAF + Azure DDoS Protection Cloud Armor is tightly integrated with Cloud LB; AWS Shield Standard is free; Shield Advanced adds cost; Azure DDoS Protection Standard applies per-VNet; all support OWASP rules and geo-blocking
CDN Cloud CDN CloudFront Azure CDN / Azure Front Door Cloud CDN uses Google's Anycast edge PoPs; CloudFront has the largest PoP count globally and Lambda@Edge for serverless at the edge; Azure Front Door combines CDN + global LB + WAF in one service
DNS Cloud DNS Route 53 Azure DNS Route 53 is the most feature-rich (health-checked routing, latency routing, geoproximity routing, alias records); Cloud DNS and Azure DNS are standard authoritative DNS; Route 53 Resolver simplifies hybrid DNS; GCP uses Cloud DNS forwarding zones for hybrid

Hybrid & Multi-Cloud Connectivity

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Dedicated private circuit Cloud Interconnect (Dedicated) AWS Direct Connect Azure ExpressRoute All three require colocation or carrier; GCP and AWS circuits are NOT encrypted by default (add VPN for encryption); Azure ExpressRoute with MACsec provides layer 2 encryption; GCP offers 10 or 100 Gbps ports; AWS offers 1–100 Gbps; Azure offers 50 Mbps to 100 Gbps via providers
Managed VPN Cloud VPN (HA VPN) AWS Site-to-Site VPN Azure VPN Gateway GCP HA VPN offers 99.99% SLA with two tunnels; max ~3 Gbps per tunnel; AWS Site-to-Site VPN max 1.25 Gbps per tunnel (ECMP for aggregation); Azure VPN Gateway up to 10 Gbps with VpnGw5 SKU

IAM & Security

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Identity & access management Cloud IAM AWS IAM Azure RBAC + Azure Active Directory (Entra ID) GCP IAM uses roles bound at project/folder/org level; AWS IAM uses policy documents attached to users/roles/resources; Azure separates Azure AD (identity) from RBAC (resource authorization); GCP Workload Identity Federation replaces service account keys for cross-cloud auth
Key management Cloud KMS / Cloud HSM AWS KMS / AWS CloudHSM Azure Key Vault (software + HSM tiers) All three offer software-backed managed keys and dedicated HSM options; GCP Cloud KMS keys are regional; AWS KMS is regional with automatic key rotation; Azure Key Vault Premium uses FIPS 140-2 Level 2 HSMs; Managed HSM is Level 3
Secrets management Secret Manager AWS Secrets Manager Azure Key Vault (Secrets) AWS Secrets Manager has native automatic rotation for RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB; GCP Secret Manager requires external rotation via Pub/Sub; Azure Key Vault handles keys, secrets, and certificates in one service
Cloud security posture Security Command Center AWS Security Hub + GuardDuty Microsoft Defender for Cloud All three aggregate findings from multiple security services; GCP SCC requires Premium for threat detection; AWS GuardDuty uses ML for threat detection; Defender for Cloud extends to on-premises and multi-cloud servers via Azure Arc

Monitoring, Logging & Observability

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Cloud monitoring Cloud Monitoring CloudWatch Metrics + Alarms Azure Monitor GCP Cloud Monitoring supports multi-cloud metrics via Ops Agent; AWS CloudWatch is deeply integrated across all AWS services; Azure Monitor is the umbrella for metrics, logs, traces, and alerts
Centralized logging Cloud Logging CloudWatch Logs Azure Monitor Logs (Log Analytics) GCP Cloud Logging exports to BigQuery for SQL-based log analytics; CloudWatch Logs Insights uses a proprietary query language; Azure Log Analytics uses KQL (Kusto Query Language)
Distributed tracing Cloud Trace AWS X-Ray Azure Application Insights All three support OpenTelemetry; AWS X-Ray uses sampling groups and service maps; Azure Application Insights is a full APM (traces, metrics, exceptions, user behavior) in one service

DevOps & IaC

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
CI/CD Cloud Build + Cloud Deploy CodeBuild + CodePipeline + CodeDeploy Azure DevOps (Pipelines) / GitHub Actions GCP Cloud Build uses YAML steps similar to GitHub Actions; Cloud Deploy adds progressive delivery to GKE/Cloud Run; AWS Code* family is tightly integrated; Azure DevOps Pipelines is mature and enterprise-proven; all three integrate with GitHub as source
Infrastructure as Code Terraform (preferred) / Config Connector / Deployment Manager (legacy) CloudFormation / Terraform / CDK ARM Templates / Bicep / Terraform GCP deprecated Deployment Manager in favor of Terraform (now an explicit PCA exam requirement); AWS CDK generates CloudFormation using TypeScript/Python; Azure Bicep is a DSL that compiles to ARM; Terraform is the multi-cloud standard across all three

AI / ML / Generative AI

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
MLOps platform Vertex AI (Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform) SageMaker Azure Machine Learning Vertex AI has the most integrated end-to-end MLOps with Google's foundation models; SageMaker has the most training job options and instance variety; Azure ML integrates with MLflow and has strong enterprise governance features; all three support Kubeflow Pipelines concepts
Foundation model / GenAI API Gemini API (Gemini 2.0/2.5 via Vertex AI) Amazon Bedrock (Claude, Llama, Titan, etc.) Azure OpenAI Service (GPT-4o, o1, etc.) GCP provides Gemini natively (Google's own models); Bedrock is a multi-model marketplace (third-party models including Anthropic Claude); Azure OpenAI is the exclusive enterprise channel for OpenAI's GPT and o-series models; Gemini has the longest context window (up to 1M tokens)
Enterprise search / RAG Vertex AI Search Amazon Kendra + Bedrock Knowledge Bases Azure AI Search (Cognitive Search) Vertex AI Search offers grounding for Gemini with minimal configuration; Amazon Kendra uses ML-based relevance; Bedrock Knowledge Bases auto-chunks and embeds documents; Azure AI Search supports hybrid (vector + keyword) search and is the most configurable of the three
Pre-trained AI APIs Cloud Vision, Natural Language, Speech-to-Text, Translation, Document AI Rekognition, Comprehend, Transcribe, Translate, Textract Azure AI Vision, Language, Speech, Translator, Form Recognizer All three offer similar pre-trained APIs; GCP Document AI is strongest for structured document extraction; AWS Textract is strong for forms/tables; Azure Form Recognizer (now Document Intelligence) has the best pre-built models for industry-specific documents

Management & Governance

Capability Google Cloud AWS Azure Notes / key difference
Resource hierarchy Resource Manager (Org > Folders > Projects) AWS Organizations (Management Account > OUs > Accounts) Azure Management Groups > Subscriptions > Resource Groups GCP projects are the billing and IAM boundary; AWS accounts are the isolation boundary; Azure subscriptions are the billing boundary with resource groups for grouping; GCP's hierarchical IAM inheritance is most straightforward for policy enforcement
Policy guardrails Organization Policy Service AWS Service Control Policies (SCPs) + AWS Config Rules Azure Policy GCP Org Policy is preventive (blocks actions); AWS SCPs set permission boundaries; Config Rules are detective (after the fact); Azure Policy supports both audit and deny effects with remediation tasks
API management Apigee / Cloud Endpoints Amazon API Gateway Azure API Management (APIM) Apigee is the most feature-rich (monetization, developer portal, analytics); AWS API Gateway tightly integrates with Lambda; Azure APIM supports multi-region and self-hosted gateway for hybrid; Cloud Endpoints is lightweight alternative to Apigee for GCP-native APIs
GCP differentiators that regularly appear on the PCA exam: Two GCP services have no true equivalents on AWS or Azure and should be understood as genuine architectural advantages: (1) Cloud Spanner — the only globally distributed relational database with external consistency and horizontal write scaling. When an exam scenario requires a globally consistent, horizontally scalable SQL database (think: global financial ledger, worldwide inventory, gaming leaderboard that must never show stale committed data), Spanner is the answer. AWS Aurora Global and Azure Cosmos DB are the closest alternatives but neither offers the same consistency guarantees with SQL semantics. (2) BigQuery — the only major cloud data warehouse that is fully serverless with no cluster to provision; query any petabyte-scale dataset in seconds, paying only for bytes scanned. Redshift Serverless exists but requires more configuration; Synapse has separate compute and storage pools to manage. When a scenario asks for a data warehouse with no infrastructure management, near-instant query scale, and built-in ML, BigQuery is the answer. Additionally, GCP's global VPC (a single VPC spanning all regions) simplifies multi-region architectures compared to AWS and Azure where VPCs/VNets are regional.
Practice

One question at a time, just like the real exam. Pick an option — it reveals the correct answer and explains every choice. Use Previous / Next (or ← → arrow keys), filter by domain, and "Try again" to redo any question.
